translation:
Wan Shijun's name was Fen, and his father was from the state of Zhao and his surname was Shi.
After the fall of Zhao State, he moved to Wen County.
Gaozu attacked Xiang Yu in the east and passed through Hanoi County. Shi Fen was fifteen years old at that time and was a minor official serving Gaozu.
Emperor Gaozu talked with him and liked his respectful and cautious attitude. He asked him: Who are the people in your family?
He replied: My mother is the only one in my family and unfortunately she is blind.
The family is very poor.
There is also a sister who can play the piano.
Gaozu asked again: Can you follow me?
He replied: I am willing to serve with all my strength.
Therefore, Emperor Gaozu called his sister into the palace to be a beauty, and asked Shi Fen to be the official in charge of receiving documents and interviews from the ministers. He moved his family to the middle family in Chang'an because his sister had become a beauty.
When Shi Fen served as an official until Emperor Xiaowen, he became a high-ranking official by accumulating merit.
He didn't know much about Confucianism, but he was as respectful and cautious as anyone else.
During the reign of Emperor Wen, Zhang Xiangru, Marquis of Dongyang, served as the Crown Prince and Tutor, but was later dismissed.
Emperor Wen chose someone who could be the prince's tutor. Everyone recommended Shi Fen, and Shi Fen became the prince's tutor.
When Emperor Jing came to the throne, he was appointed to the position of Jiuqing. Because he was too respectful and cautious and approached him, Emperor Jing was also afraid of him and made him the prime minister of the princes.
His eldest son Shi Jian, his second son Shi Jia, his third son Shi Yi, and his fourth son Shi Qing all had official positions of two thousand shi because of their docile temperament, respect for their elders, and caution in doing things. So Emperor Jing said: "Shi Jun and his four sons all have official positions of two thousand shi, and the honor and glory of being ministers is concentrated in their family."
He called Shi Fen the King of Wan Shi.
In the last years of Emperor Jing's reign, Wan Shijun enjoyed the salary of a doctor and returned home in old age. When the imperial court held grand ceremonies and issued orders, he attended as a minister.
When passing the palace gate, Wan Shijun must get out of the car and walk quickly to show respect. When seeing the emperor's driver, he must put his hand on the driver's chariot to express his greetings.
When his descendants and grandchildren serve as minor officials and come home to visit him, Wan Shijun must wear court clothes to receive them and not call them by their names.
When some of his descendants made mistakes, he would not scold them. Instead, he would sit on a side seat and face the table and refuse to eat.
After this, other descendants will blame the person who made the mistake one after another, and then ask for mercy from the elders of the clan. They will bare their upper body to express their apology and resolutely correct themselves, and then they will agree to their request.
When his adult children and grandchildren are around him, he must dress neatly and show a serious and tidy look, even if he is staying at home.
His servants are also very respectful and cautious.
Sometimes when the emperor would reward food and send it to his home, he would always kowtow and bow before eating it, just like in front of the emperor.
When he was attending the funeral, he was very sad and mournful.
Future generations will follow his teachings and do as he did.
Wan Shijun's family was famous in all counties and vassal states for their filial piety and prudence. Even the simple Confucian scholars in Qi and Lu thought that they were inferior to them.
In the second year of Jianyuan, Wang Zang, the doctor's order, was convicted for advocating Confucianism.
The Empress Dowager believed that most of the Confucian scholars' speeches were exaggerated and not simple enough. Now Wan Shijun's family did not talk much but practiced it. She made Wan Shijun's eldest son Shi Jian the doctor's order and his youngest son Shi Qing the internal historian.
Shi Jian's hair turned gray in his old age, but Wan Shijun's body was still healthy and disease-free.
As a doctor, Shi Jian took one day off every five days. When he went home to visit his father, he first entered the waiter's cabin, privately asked the waiter about his father, took his underwear and washed it himself in the ditch outside the door, and then handed it to the waiter. He did not dare to let his father know that he often did this.
When Shi Jian was a doctor, he had something to say to the emperor. When he could avoid others, he spoke freely and urgently;
Therefore, the emperor showed respect and courtesy to him personally.
Wan Shijun moved to Lingli.
Shi Qing, the son of the internal historian, returned drunk and did not get out of the car when he entered the inner gate.
Wan Shijun refused to eat after hearing this.
Shi Qing was frightened and bared his upper body to ask for forgiveness, but Wan Shijun still refused.
The whole clan and his brother Shi Jian also exposed their upper bodies and begged for forgiveness. Wan Shijun scolded him and said: "Nei Shi is a noble person. When he entered the inner gate, the elders in the inner city hurriedly avoided him, but Nei Shi sat in the car and remained the same, not knowing how to restrain himself. This is what he should do!"
After saying that, he ordered Shi Qing to go away.
From then on, when Shi Qing and his brothers entered the inner gate, they all got out of the car and walked home quickly.
Wan Shijun died in the fifth year of Emperor Wu Yuanshuo.
The eldest son, Lang Zhongling, Shi Jian cried so much that he could only walk with a cane. More than a year later, Shi Jian also passed away.
Wan Shijun's descendants are all filial, but Shi Jian stands out the most, surpassing Wan Shijun.
When Shi Jian was the doctor, he wrote a memorial once and approved it. When Shi Jian read it again, he said in horror: "Wrong writing!" The four points under the character "horse" and the horsetail under the curve should be five strokes, but now there are only four strokes. One stroke is missing. If the emperor finds out and blames him, he will die!
This shows that he is very cautious even when dealing with other things.
Wan Shijun's youngest son Shi Qing was a servant and was driving for the emperor. The emperor asked him how many horses he was driving. After Shi Qing counted the horses one by one with a whip, he raised his hand and said: six.
Shi Qing is the most casual among his sons, yet he is still so cautious.
Shi Qing became the Prime Minister of Qi State, and everyone in Qi State admired their family tradition, so Qi State was very stable without issuing decrees, so people built the Shixiang Temple in Shi Qing's honor.
In the first year of Emperor Wu's Yuanshou, the emperor established the prince and selected people who could be the prince's teachers from among the ministers. Shi Qing was promoted from the post of Pei Taishou to the prince's tutor, and seven years later he was promoted to the imperial censor.
In the autumn of the fifth year of Emperor Wu's Yuanding reign, Prime Minister Zhao Zhou was dismissed from office for a crime.
The emperor issued an edict to the imperial censor doctor: The late emperor respected Wan Shijun very much, and their descendants were very filial. He ordered the censor doctor Shi Qing to serve as prime minister and be granted the title of Marquis Muqiu.
At this time, the imperial court was conquering South Vietnam and East Vietnam in the south, attacking North Korea in the east, expelling the Xiongnu in the north, and conquering Dawan in the west. The country was in a troubled period.
In addition, the emperor visited various parts of the country, repaired ancient temples, went to Mount Tai to worship heaven, and to Liangfu to worship the land, and promoted rituals and music.
When the country's finances were in trouble, the emperor asked Sang Hongyang and others to seek financial gain, Wang Wenshu and others implemented strict laws, and made Erkuan and others respect Confucianism. They all reached the rank of Jiuqing, and were promoted to power alternately. Major affairs in the court did not depend on the prime minister, who was just loyal and prudent.
Shi Qing had been in power for nine years, but he had not been able to make any remarks to rectify the current situation. He once wanted to punish the emperor's close ministers Zhong Zhong and Jiuqing Xianxuan for their sins. Not only could they not plead guilty, but they were punished.
In the fourth year of the Yuanfeng reign of Emperor Wu of the Han Dynasty, two million people in Guandong were displaced, and 400,000 people were without household registration. The ministers discussed and asked the emperor to move the refugees to the frontiers as a way to punish them.
The emperor believed that the prime minister was too old and cautious to participate in such discussions, so he asked him to take leave and go home, while the officials below the imperial censor who made such requests were investigated.
The prime minister felt guilty because he was not qualified for his position, so he wrote to the emperor and said: I, Shi Qing, have been favored to be prime minister, but my ability is too poor to assist your majesty in governing the country. As a result, the warehouses in the suburbs are empty and the people are displaced. I deserve death. The emperor cannot bear to punish me according to law. I am willing to return the seals of the prime minister and the marquis, request to retire and return home, and make way for a worthy person.
The emperor said: The granaries are empty, the people are poor and displaced, but you want to ask them to be relocated. The society is already in turmoil. The social turmoil has put the country in crisis. At this time, you want to resign from your position. Who do you want to blame?
He blamed Shi Qing with an edict. Shi Qing was very ashamed, and then he went back to handling political affairs.
Shi Qing was thoughtful and cautious, but he did not have any brilliant insights or the performance of speaking for the people.
More than three years have passed since then. In the second year of Taichu, Prime Minister Shi Qing passed away and was given the posthumous title Tianhou.
Shi Qing's second son was Ming De. Shi Qing liked and valued him very much. The emperor asked Shi De to be Shi Qing's heir and inherit the title of marquis.
Later I did it too often. Because he violated the law and was sentenced to death, Namsu became an official and became a civilian after atonement.
When Shi Qing was prime minister, thirteen of his descendants were promoted from minor officials to the rank of two thousand shi.
After Shi Qing's death, he was gradually removed from office for different crimes, and the family tradition of filial piety and prudence also declined.
Wei Wan, Marquis of Jianling, was a native of Daling, Daijun.
Wei Wan became a Langguan by virtue of his driving skills and served Emperor Wen. He was promoted to Zhonglangjiang due to his continuous meritorious service. He had nothing but loyalty and prudence.
When Emperor Jing was the crown prince, he invited the emperor's closest officials to a banquet, but Wei Wan refused to go because he was ill.
When Emperor Wen was about to die, he told Emperor Jing: Wei Wan is a loyal man, you should treat him well.
When Emperor Wen died and Emperor Jing came to the throne, Emperor Jing did not reprimand Wei Wan for more than a year. Wei Wan just performed his duties more cautiously day by day.
Emperor Jing once visited Shanglinyuan and ordered Wei Wan, the lieutenant general, to ride in a carriage with him. When he came back, he asked Wei Wan: Do you know why you can ride in the same carriage with me?
Wei Wan said: From a small charioteer, I was lucky enough to be promoted to a lieutenant general due to my meritorious service. I don’t know why.
Emperor Jing asked again: When I became the crown prince, I invited you to a banquet, but you refused to come. Why?
He replied: I deserve to die, I was really sick at that time!
Emperor Jing gave him a sword.
Wei Wan said: The late Emperor once gave me a total of six swords. I dare not accept any more rewards from His Majesty.
Emperor Jing said: Swords are a favorite thing among people and are often used as gifts or in exchange for other things. How can you keep it until now? Wei Wan said: They are all still there.
The emperor sent someone to fetch the six swords. The swords were intact in their sheaths and had never been used.
When the Lang officers under the Zhong Lang general made mistakes, Wei Wan would often accept the consequences for them without arguing with others; when he had merit, he would always humbly give it to others.
The emperor believed that he was of upright character and loyal to himself, so he appointed him as the Taifu of Liu De, King of Hejian.
During the Seven Kingdoms Rebellion between Wu and Chu, the emperor appointed Wei Wan as a general. He led the army of the King of Hejian to attack the rebels of Wu and Chu, and made him a lieutenant.
Three years later, because of his military exploits, he was granted the title of Marquis of Jianling in the sixth year of Emperor Jing's reign.
The next year, Emperor Jing deposed Prince Li Liu Rong and killed the prince's uncle and others.
Emperor Jing thought Wei Wan was a loyal person and couldn't bear to let him handle this big case, so he gave him leave to go home. And let Zhi Du arrest and interrogate the Li clan members.
After handling this case, Emperor Jing appointed Liu Che, King of Jiaodong, as the crown prince, recruited Wei Wan, and appointed him as the crown prince and tutor.
After a long time, he was promoted to the imperial censor.
Five years later, he replaced Liu She, Taohou, as prime minister, and only reported matters within his duties to the court.
However, from the time he first became an official until he became prime minister, there was nothing to praise or blame.
The emperor thought that he was kind-hearted and could assist the young master. He respected and loved him very much and rewarded him with many things.
Wei Wan served as prime minister for three years, Emperor Jing died, and Emperor Wu ascended the throne.
During the Jianyuan period, when Emperor Jing was ill, many of the prisoners in various government offices were innocent people who had been wronged. As prime minister, he failed to fulfill his duties and was dismissed from his position as prime minister.
Later, Wei Wan died, and his son Wei Xin inherited the title of Marquis of Jianling.
Later, he lost his title because the aide's donation was irregular. There is no doubt that Saihou is from Nanyang.
He served Emperor Wen as a Langguan.
The person who lived in the same room with him asked for leave to visit his family and mistakenly took other people's gold. After some time, the owner of the gold found out and made random suspicions about Zhi Buyi. Zhi Buyi apologized to him and admitted the incident, and bought gold to repay him.
When the person who took leave to visit the family came back and returned the gold, the person who had lost the gold was extremely ashamed, so people said that he was undoubtedly an honest person.
Emperor Wen also praised and promoted him, and he was gradually promoted to Taizhong doctor.
One time when he went to the court, someone slandered him and said: "Zhi is very beautiful, but there is no way to deal with the fact that he likes to have an affair with his sister-in-law!"
After Zhi Buyi heard about it, he said, "I don't have an elder brother."
After all, he made no other excuses.
During the Seven Kingdoms Rebellion between Wu and Chu, Zhi Busuo led troops to attack the rebels with an official position of two thousand stones.
In the first year of Emperor Jing's reign, he was appointed as the imperial censor.
Emperor Jing commended the meritorious personnel who put down the rebellion of Wu and Chu, and made Zhi Busuo the Marquis of Sai.
During the Jianyuan period of Emperor Wu, both he and Prime Minister Wei Wan were dismissed from their official posts due to negligence.
I have no doubts about studying Laozi’s teachings.
When he governed every place, he held official positions in the same way as his predecessors, lest people know his official deeds.
He doesn't like to build up his reputation and be called an elder.
After Zhi Buyi's death, his son Xiangru inherited the position of marquis.
When the grandson was looking forward to it, he lost his position as marquis because the aide's donation did not meet the requirements.
The doctor named Zhou Wen was named Ren, and his ancestors were originally from Rencheng.
Relying on his medical skills to visit the emperor.
When Emperor Jing became the crown prince, he appointed him as a minister. His accumulated merits gradually improved, and during the reign of Emperor Wen, he became a senior official in Taizhong.
As soon as Emperor Jing succeeded to the throne, he appointed Zhou Ren as doctor.
Zhou Ren was very reserved and did not reveal other people's words. He often wore shabby and patched clothes and underwear that could absorb urine, deliberately making his clothes unclean, so that the concubines would not come close to him, so he was favored by Emperor Jing.
When Emperor Jing entered the palace and played obscenely with his concubines, Zhou Ren was often by his side.
When Emperor Jing died, Zhou Ren was still serving as the doctor, but he never said anything.
Sometimes when the emperor asked about other people's situation, Zhou Ren always said: The emperor should examine him personally.
But he didn't say anything bad to others.
Therefore, Emperor Jing visited his home many times, and his family later moved to Yangling.
The emperor rewarded many things, but he often declined and did not dare to accept them.
He never accepted the gifts from the princes and officials.
When Emperor Wu of the Han Dynasty came to the throne, he considered him a minister of the late emperor and respected him.
Zhou Ren was dismissed from his post due to illness and the court allowed him to enjoy a salary of two thousand dan per year to return to his hometown for retirement. His descendants all became high officials.
Uncle Zhang, the imperial censor, was named Ou, and he was the concubine of Zhang Shuo, the Marquis of Anqiu.
During the reign of Emperor Wen, he served the prince by studying Legalist theory.
Although Zhang Ou studied Legalism, he was very loyal.
During the reign of Emperor Jing, he was very respected and was often ranked among the nine ministers.
In the fourth year of Yuanshuo, Emperor Wu, Han Anguo was dismissed, and the emperor appointed Zhang Ou as the imperial censor.
Since Zhang Ou became an official, he has never said anything about punishing people. He has been an official with honesty and loyalty.
His subordinates all thought that he was a loyal person and did not dare to deceive him too much.
The emperor handed over the cases to be tried to him, and if they could be returned for retrial, he would return them; if they could not be returned for retrial, he had no choice but to look at the sealed documents with tears in his eyes.
This is how he loves and cares for others.
Later, he became old and seriously ill and asked to be removed from his official position.
The emperor also issued an edict, granted his request, and allowed him to return to his hometown for retirement according to the doctor's salary. He lives in Yangling.
His descendants have all become high officials.
Tai Shigong said: Confucius once said: A gentleman should be slow in speech but quick in action. This sentence refers to Lord Wan Shi, Marquis Jianling and Uncle Zhang!
Therefore, they are not hurried but can make things successful, and their measures are not harsh but can make society stable.
Saihou Zhibu was too cunning and deceitful, but Zhou Wen failed to be humble and flattering. Gentlemen ridiculed them because they were close to flattery.
But they can also be regarded as gentlemen with honest behavior.
summary:
The text recounts the lives of several officials from the Han Dynasty, focusing on their conduct and family traditions. Wan Shijun, originally from Zhao, served under Emperor Gaozu and rose through ranks due to his respectful and cautious nature. He became a tutor to the prince and later a high-ranking official, with his four sons also holding significant posts. His family was renowned for filial piety and prudence, influencing even Confucian scholars. Wan Shijun’s strict household rules included requiring descendants to show respect, and he would refuse to eat if they erred. His son Shi Jian was notably filial, washing his father’s clothes secretly and being meticulous in his duties. Another son, Shi Qing, became prime minister but struggled during turbulent times, eventually dying in office. The family’s tradition declined after his death.

Wei Wan, Marquis of Jianling, served Emperors Wen and Jing with loyalty and prudence. He refused a banquet invitation from the future Emperor Jing, claiming illness, and kept six swords given by Emperor Wen unused. He took blame for subordinates’ mistakes and deflected credit. During the Seven Kingdoms Rebellion, he led troops and was made marquis. He later became prime minister but was dismissed for failing to address injustices during Emperor Jing’s illness.

Zhi Buyi, Marquis of Sai, was known for honesty, once apologizing for a mistaken accusation of theft. He served as imperial censor and was dismissed during Emperor Wu’s reign. He followed Laozi’s teachings and governed without seeking fame.

Zhou Wen, a doctor, served Emperor Jing with reserve, wearing shabby clothes to avoid concubines. He never revealed others’ words and declined gifts. He retired with a salary under Emperor Wu.

Zhang Ou, imperial censor, studied Legalism but was loyal and merciful, often weeping over cases he could not retry. He retired due to illness.

The historian concludes that Wan Shijun, Wei Wan, and Zhang Ou exemplified Confucian ideals of cautious speech and action, while Zhi Buyi and Zhou Wen, though flawed, were still honest gentlemen.
rel_path: 史记/七十列传/万石张叔列传

translation:
King Wuzhu of Minyue was the same as King Yao of Yuedonghai. Their ancestors were all descendants of King Goujian of Yue, and their surname was Zou.
After the Qin Dynasty annexed the world, they all had their royal titles abolished and became rulers, and their place was set up as Minzhong County.
When the princes rebelled against the Qin Dynasty, Wu Zhuheyao led the Yue people to join Wu Rui, the magistrate of Poyang County, who was known as Pojun, and followed the princes to destroy the Qin Dynasty.
At that time, Xiang Ji had the power to command the princes and did not make Wu Zhu and Yao kings. Therefore, they did not join the King of Chu.
The King of Han attacked Xiang Ji, but Wu Zhu and Yao led the Yue people to assist the King of Han.
In the fifth year of the Han Dynasty, Wu Zhu was re-established as the King of Minyue. He was proclaimed king in the original place of Minyue and established his capital in Dongye.
In the third year of Emperor Hui of the Han Dynasty, he listed the contributions of the Yue people during the reign of Emperor Gao. The court believed that Lord Yao of Fujian had the most credit and his people were willing to join him, so Yao was appointed King of Donghai and established his capital in Dongou. The world called him King of Dongou.
Several generations later, in the third year of Emperor Jing of the Han Dynasty, King Wu Liu Bi rebelled and wanted Minyue to follow him to rebel against the Han Dynasty. Minyue was unwilling to follow, so only Dongou followed King Wu to rebel.
When King Wu failed, Dong Ou accepted the heavy bribes from the Han Dynasty and killed King Wu Liu Bi in Dantu. Therefore, he was not killed and returned to his own country.
Ziju, the son of King Wu Liu Bi, fled to Minyue. He resented Dongou for killing his father and often persuaded Minyue to attack Dongou.
In the third year of the founding of Emperor Wu of the Han Dynasty, Minyue sent troops to besiege Dongou.
Dongou ran out of food and was in great distress. He was about to surrender, so he sent someone to ask for help from the Emperor of Han.
The emperor asked Tian Fu about this, and Tian Fu replied: It is common for the Yue people to attack each other, and their attitudes are capricious. It is not worth bothering the Central Plains court to come to the rescue.
From the Qin Dynasty, they began to abandon them and did not treat them as subordinate countries.
So Zhuang Zhu, the Chinese doctor, questioned Tian Fu and said: "I am just worried that my strength is not enough to save them, and my kindness is shallow and cannot cover them. If you really have the ability to save them, why abandon them?"
Moreover, the Qin State even abandoned the entire Xianyang, so how could it just abandon the Yue State?
Nowadays, when small countries encounter difficulties and have no other choice, they come to the emperor for help. If the emperor does not come to rescue them, where will they go to complain and ask for help?
How can the emperor raise and protect the people of all nations?
The emperor said: The captain's opinion is not worthy of discussion.
I had just ascended the throne, and I didn't want to take out the tiger talisman to mobilize troops from the county to fight.
So he sent Zhuangzhu to Kuaiji with the talisman to mobilize troops for the expedition.
The prefect of Kuaiji wanted to resist the order and refused to send troops to Zhuang Zhu for the expedition. Zhuang Zhu killed an army commander and clearly stated the emperor's will, and then the prefect of Kuaiji sent troops from the sea to rescue Dong Ou.
Before the army reached Dongou, Minyue led his troops to evacuate.
Dong Ou requested that the entire country be moved to areas that the imperial court could directly manage, so he led all the people to move here and live in the Jianghuai area.
In the sixth year of Jianyuan, Fujian and Vietnam attacked South Vietnam.
South Vietnam abided by the emperor's restrictions and did not dare to send troops to fight back without authorization, but reported the matter to the emperor.
The emperor sent Wang Hui, a great general, to lead his troops from Yuzhang, and Han Anguo, a great farmer, from Kuaiji. They both served as generals.
Before their army crossed the Yangshan Mountains, King Ying of Minyue sent troops to guard the dangerous place to fight against the Han army.
Ying's younger brother Yu Shan discussed with the Prime Minister of Dongyue and the clan members and said: Our king sent troops to attack South Vietnam without authorization without asking the emperor for instructions, so the emperor sent troops to attack.
Now that the Han Dynasty's army is numerous and powerful, if we can defeat them by luck now, the emperor will definitely send more troops in the future until our country is wiped out.
Now if we kill the king and apologize to the emperor, if the emperor accepts our request, the war will be stopped and our country will be preserved intact. If the Emperor does not accept our apology, we will fight hard; if we cannot win, we will flee to the sea. Everyone said: Good attention!
So he killed Ying with a spear and halberd, and sent an envoy with his head to Daxing Wang Hui.
Wang Hui said: Our army came here to kill the King of Dongyue. Now that the head of the King of Dongyue has been delivered, Dongyue has also apologized. The disaster has been eliminated without fighting. There is no greater benefit than this.
He stopped the military operation in a flexible and convenient way, informed Han Anguo of the situation, and sent an envoy to Chang'an with the head of the King of Dongyue to report to the emperor.
The emperor issued an edict to stop the military operations of Wang Hui and Han Anguo's troops, saying: Wang Ying and others of Dongyue were the first to commit evil, and only Wu Zhu's grandson Yao Junchou did not participate in this conspiracy.
The emperor then sent a lieutenant general to establish Chou as the king of Yue Yao to perform the sacrificial rituals to the king of Minyue.
After Yu Shan killed Ying, his prestige spread throughout the country, and most of the people in the country belonged to him, so he secretly proclaimed himself king.
King Yao could not correct the mistakes of his people and keep them on the right path.
After the emperor heard this, he thought it was not worth raising troops for Yu Shan's cause, saying: "Yu Shan conspired with Ying many times to cause trouble, and later killed Ying first, so that the Han army could avoid hardship."
So he appointed Yu Shan as the king of Dongyue and lived with King Yao at the same time.
In the fifth year of Yuanding, South Vietnam rebelled. King Yu Shan of East Vietnam wrote to the Emperor of the Han Dynasty, requesting that he lead eight thousand soldiers to follow General Lou Chuan to attack Lu Jia and others.
When his army arrived in Jieyang, he used the storm at sea as an excuse to stop advancing and adopted a wait-and-see attitude. He secretly sent people to contact South Vietnam.
When the Han army captured Panyu, the East Vietnamese army did not come.
At this time, Yang Pu, the general of Louzhou, sent an envoy to write to the emperor, willing to lead troops to attack East Vietnam.
The emperor said that the soldiers were tired and tired, so he did not approve the request of General Lou Chuan. He stopped the military operation and ordered the officers to garrison at Meiling in Yuzhang to wait for orders.
In the autumn of the sixth year of Yuan Ding, Yu Shan heard that General Lou Chuan requested to attack him, and that the Han army had advanced to the border of East Vietnam and was about to attack, so he rebelled and sent troops to resist the Han army's only route.
He also gave General Zou Li and others the official title of "General Tun Han". The army entered Baisha, Wulin and Meiling and killed three captains of the Han army.
At this time, the Han Dynasty sent Zhang Cheng, a great farmer, and Liu Chi, the former Shanzhou Marquis, to lead their troops to garrison here. They did not dare to attack the East Vietnamese army and retreated to a favorable place. They were all executed for their cowardice and fear of the enemy.
Yu Shan carved the seal of Emperor Wu and proclaimed himself emperor, deceiving his people and spreading absurd remarks.
The emperor of the Han Dynasty sent General Han Shuo of Henghai to set out from Juzhang and cross the sea to march from the east; General Yang Pu of Louchuan set off from Wulin; Lieutenant Wang Wenshu set out from Meiling; two Yue people who had surrendered to the Han Dynasty and were made marquises became General Gechuan and General Xiase, and they set out from Ruoye and Baisha.
In the winter of the first year of Yuanfeng, all these generals led their troops into East Vietnam.
Dongyue has been sending troops to defend dangerous places. It sent General Lianbei to guard Wulin, defeated several lieutenants of General Louchuan, and killed the chief officials.
General Lou Chuan led Qiantang people Yuan Zhonggu to kill General Yibei and was named Yu'erhou.
His own army did not go to Wulin.
The original Yue Yanhou Wu Yang had stayed in the Han Dynasty before that, and the Han Dynasty sent him back to East Yue to persuade Yu Shan. Yu Shan refused to obey.
When Han Yue, the general of Henghai, led his troops to East Vietnam, Wu Yang, the Marquis of Yueyan, led 700 people in his town to rebel against East Vietnam and attacked the East Vietnam army in Hanyang.
He discussed with Jiancheng Hou Ao and his men, and King Yao Jugu, saying: "Yu Shan was the first to cause trouble and hijack us people."
Now that the Han Dynasty's army has arrived, they are numerous and powerful. We have planned to kill Yu Shan, and the generals who have surrendered to the Han Dynasty may be able to get away with their sins.
So everyone killed Yu Shan together and led their soldiers to surrender to General Henghai. Therefore, the Han Dynasty granted King Yao Jugu the title of Marquis of Dongcheng, with a settlement of 10,000 households; the title of Marquis of Chengcheng, Ao, became the Marquis of Kailing; the title of Marquis of Yue Yan, Wu Yang, was the title of Beishi Marquis; the title of General Han Yuo of Henghai was made the Marquis of Jidao; and the title of Liu Fu, the captain of Henghai School, was the title of Marquis of Liaoqi.
Liu Fu is the son of Liu happy, the co-king of Chengyang. He was originally a Marquis of Haichang, but he lost the Marquis title for breaking the law.
In the past, I did not achieve military merit when I joined the army. I was granted the title of Marquis because I was a descendant of the clan.
The other generals had no military exploits, so they were not awarded the title.
The general of East Vietnam had many armies. When the Han army arrived, he gave up his army and surrendered, so he was named Marquis of Wuxi.
So the Emperor of the Han Dynasty said that Dongyue was a small and dangerous place, while Minyue was strong and capricious. Therefore, the officers were ordered to lead all the East Vietnamese people to migrate to the Jianghuai area.
East Vietnam has become a place of emptiness.
Tai Shigong said: Although the Yue Kingdom was a barbarian, did its ancestors ever have great merits for the people? Otherwise, why has it been passed down from generation to generation for so long?
After several generations, he was always the king, but Gou Jian was once the hegemon.
However, Yu Shan actually did something treacherous and unethical. The country was wiped out and the people were relocated. The descendants of their ancestors, King Yao Jugu and others, were also granted the title of Marquis of Wanhu. It can be seen from this that Dongyue has been a duke for generations.
Perhaps this is the legacy left by Dayu.
summary:
The text recounts the history of the Minyue and Dongou kingdoms, descendants of King Goujian of Yue, with the surname Zou. After the Qin Dynasty annexed their lands, they were demoted to rulers and their territory became Minzhong County. During the rebellion against Qin, Wu Zhu and Yao led the Yue people to join Wu Rui and helped destroy Qin, but Xiang Ji refused to make them kings, so they later supported the King of Han against Xiang Ji. In the fifth year of the Han Dynasty, Wu Zhu was reinstated as King of Minyue, with his capital at Dongye. In the third year of Emperor Hui's reign, Yao was made King of Donghai, known as King of Dongou, for his contributions.

During Emperor Jing's reign, King Wu Liu Bi of Wu rebelled. Minyue refused to join, but Dongou followed. After Wu's defeat, Dongou killed him in Dantu under Han bribery and returned home. Liu Bi's son, Ziju, fled to Minyue and urged attacks on Dongou. In the third year of Emperor Wu's reign, Minyue besieged Dongou. Dongou sought Han aid; after debate, Zhuang Zhu was sent to mobilize troops from Kuaiji. Before the army arrived, Minyue withdrew. Dongou then relocated its people to the Jianghuai region.

In the sixth year of Jianyuan, Minyue attacked South Vietnam. The Han emperor sent generals Wang Hui and Han Anguo to intervene. King Ying of Minyue resisted, but his brother Yu Shan killed him and surrendered, ending the conflict. Yu Shan later proclaimed himself king, and the emperor appointed him King of Dongyue alongside King Yao. In the fifth year of Yuanding, Yu Shan feigned support for a Han campaign against South Vietnam but secretly aided the enemy. After the Han victory, General Yang Pu sought to attack Dongyue, but the emperor initially declined. In the sixth year of Yuanding, Yu Shan rebelled, attacked Han forces, and proclaimed himself emperor. The Han sent multiple generals to suppress him. Yu Shan was killed by his own people, including King Yao Jugu, who surrendered. The Han emperor then relocated the Dongyue population to the Jianghuai region, leaving the area empty. Tai Shigong reflects on the Yue kingdom's longevity, attributing it to ancestral merits and the legacy of Dayu, despite Yu Shan's treachery leading to its downfall.

translation:
Le Yi, his ancestor was named Le Yang.
Leyang once served as the general of Wei Wenhou and once led troops to capture the Zhongshan Kingdom. Wei Wenhou gave Lingshou as a fief to Leyang.
After Le Yang died, he was buried in Lingshou, and his descendants settled there.
Later, Zhongshan was restored to the state, and it was destroyed by King Wuling of Zhao, and the descendants of the Le family gave birth to a famous man named Le Yi.
Le Yi was a virtuous man who loved military affairs. The people of Zhao once recommended him to serve as an official.
After King Wu Ling was besieged and starved to death in his palace in the sand dunes, he left Zhao and went to Wei.
Later, he heard that King Yan Zhao was defeated by Qi due to the chaos in Yan State due to his son's rule. Therefore, King Yan Zhao hated Qi State and never forgot to take revenge on Qi State.
The State of Yan was a weak country, located in a remote area, and its national power could not defeat the enemy, so King Zhao of Yan lowered his status and treated virtuous corporals. He first treated Guo Wei with thoughtful etiquette, so as to recruit more virtuous people.
At this time, Le Yi came to Yan State as an envoy for King Zhao of Wei, and King Yan received him with the courtesy of a guest.
Le Yi refused to give in, and finally presented a gift to King Yan Zhao to express his willingness to dedicate himself as a minister. King Yan Zhao appointed him as Yaqing, a position he held for a long time.
At that time, King Min of Qi was very powerful. In the south, he defeated Chu Prime Minister Tang Wei at Chongqiu, and in the west, he defeated Wei and Zhao at Guanjin. He then united with Han, Zhao and Wei to attack Qin. He also helped Zhao to destroy Zhongshan and defeated Song, which increased Qi's territory by more than a thousand miles.
He and King Zhao of Qin jointly fought for the title of emperor. Soon after, he canceled the title of Eastern Emperor on his own and still claimed the title of king.
All the vassal states planned to desert Qin and submit to Qi.
However, King Qi Min was very arrogant and arrogant, and the people could no longer tolerate it.
King Yan Zhao thought that the opportunity to attack Qi had come, so he asked Le Yi about attacking Qi.
Le Yi replied: Qi State was originally a hegemonic country and still retains its foundation. This country has a vast land and a large population. We cannot easily attack it alone.
If the king must attack it, it is better to unite Zhao, Chu and Wei to attack it.
So King Yan Zhao sent Le Yi to form an alliance and treaty with King Zhao Huiwen, and also sent envoys to unite Chu and Wei, and asked Zhao to persuade Qin with the benefit of attacking Qi.
Because the princes believed that King Qi Min was arrogant and tyrannical, which would eventually be harmful to them, they all rushed to join forces with Yan to attack Qi.
Le Yi came back to report on his mission. King Yan Zhao mobilized the country's troops and sent Le Yi as general. King Zhao Huiwen awarded Le Yi the seal of the Prime Minister.
Le Yi then unified the armies of Zhao, Chu, Han, Wei, and Yan to attack Qi, and defeated the Qi army west of Jishui.
At this time, the armies of various princes stopped attacking and retreated to their own countries. The Yan army, under the command of Le Yi, pursued the fleeing enemy alone until they reached Linzi, the capital of Qi.
After King Min of Qi was defeated west of Jishui, he fled to Juyi and held the city.
Le Yi stayed alone and led troops to patrol the occupied areas. All the cities in Qi State held fast and refused to surrender.
Le Yi concentrated his forces to attack Linzi. After taking Linzi, he seized all the treasures and property of Qi and the ritual objects of the ancestral temple and transported them to Yan.
King Yan Zhao was overjoyed and rushed to the shore of Jishui to express his condolences to the army. He rewarded and rewarded the soldiers with wine and meat. He granted the Chang Kingdom to Leyi and named him the King of Chang Kingdom.
At that time, King Yan Zhao brought the spoils captured in Qi State back to Yan State, and asked Le Yi to continue to lead troops to attack the Qi State cities that had not yet been captured.
Le Yi stayed in the Qi State for five years and patrolled and fought. He captured more than 70 cities and towns in the Qi State. They were all classified as counties and belonged to the Yan State. Only Ju and Jimo were not conquered.
At this time, King Zhao of Yan died, and his son became King Hui of Yan.
King Hui had been dissatisfied with Le Yi since he was the crown prince. After he came to the throne, Tian Dan of Qi State learned that he had conflicts with Le Yi, so he implemented counterintuitive tactics against Yan State and spread rumors that there were only two cities in Qi State that were not captured.
The reason for the delay in conquering was said to be that Le Yi had a grudge against the new king of Yan State. Le Yi used troops intermittently to deliberately delay the time and stay in Qi State, preparing to proclaim himself king in Qi State.
What the people of Qi were most worried about was that King Yan would send a general to replace Le Yi.
At that time, King Yan Hui had already suspected Le Yi, and was provoked by Qi's counterintuitive plot, so he sent Qi Jie to replace Le Yi as general and recalled Le Yi.
Le Yi knew in his heart that King Yan Hui had bad intentions in sending someone to replace him. He was afraid that he would be killed when he returned home, so he went west and surrendered to Zhao.
The State of Zhao granted the place of Guanjin to Le Yi, with the title Wangzhujun.
The State of Zhao respected Le Yi very much and used it as a favor to shock and intimidate the States of Yan and Qi.
Tian Dan of the Qi State later fought against the Qi Jie, and he indeed set up a trap to confuse the Yan army. As a result, the Qi Jie army was defeated at the gate of Jimo City. He then chased the Yan army in many battles and chased north to the Yellow River. He recovered all the cities of the Qi State and welcomed King Xiang of Qi back to the capital Linzi from Juyi.
King Yan Hui regretted that he sent cavalry to replace Le Yi, which caused the Yan army to be defeated and lost the captured land of Qi. However, he also resented Le Yi for surrendering to Zhao and worried that Zhao would use Le Yi to take advantage of Yan's defeat and fatigue to attack Yan.
King Yan Hui sent someone to Zhao to blame Le Yi and apologize to him, saying: The late king entrusted the entire Yan State to the general. The general defeated Qi for the Yan State and avenged the late king. Everyone in the world was shocked. How can I dare to forget the general's achievements one day!
Just when the late king passed away, I came to the throne for the first time, but the people around me delayed me.
The reason why I sent the cavalry to replace the general is because the general has been away from home all year round and slept in the open air. Therefore, I recalled the general to rest for a while and discuss the government affairs together.
I didn't want the general to misunderstand the rumors and think that there was something incompatible with me, so he abandoned the state of Yan and joined the state of Zhao.
It's okay for the general to plan for himself, but how can he cope with the deep affection that the late king showed towards the general?
Le Yi wrote a reply to King Hui, saying:
I am incapable of obeying your orders and obeying the wishes of those around you. I am afraid that something unexpected will happen when I return home, which will damage the wisdom of the late king and your moral character. Therefore, I ran away from Qi and defected to Zhao.
Now you have sent someone to accuse me of my sins. I am afraid that the late king's attendants cannot understand the reason why the late king took me in and favored me, and they are not clear about my sincerity in serving the former king, so I take the liberty to answer your question with a letter.
I have heard that a wise and capable monarch will not reward those close to him with titles and salaries, but will reward those who have made more achievements and promote those who are capable.
Therefore, those who examine talents and then grant official positions are monarchs who can achieve great achievements. A person who makes friends only after measuring his character is a wise man who can build up his reputation.
I secretly observed the behavior of the late king and saw that he had ambitions that were beyond ordinary monarchs, so I took advantage of the opportunity of being an envoy to the State of Wei to go to the State of Yan to offer myself for inspection.
The late king paid special tribute to me. He first included me among the guests, and then selected me to be higher than the other ministers. He appointed me as Yaqing without consulting the elders in the royal family.
I also lacked self-awareness. I thought that as long as I carried out orders and accepted instruction, I would be able to avoid committing crimes, so I accepted the appointment without refusing.
The late king instructed me: I have accumulated hatred against Qi for a long time. I hate Qi deeply. I don't care about the weakness of Yan, but I want to take revenge on Qi as my own business.
I said: That Qi State still retains the foundation of a hegemony, and has the experience of winning many battles.
The soldiers are well-trained and proficient in offensive strategies.
If the king wants to attack it, he must unite with the princes of the world to plot it.
If you want to plot with the princes of the world, there is no one more suitable than Zhao State.
Moreover, Huaibei and the area that originally belonged to the Song State were areas that Chu and Wei wanted respectively. If Zhao agreed to form an alliance, they would make an appointment with the four countries to jointly attack it, so that Qi State could be completely defeated.
The late king thought that my idea was right, so he prepared a talisman and sent me south to the State of Zhao.
Soon I returned to my country and resumed my duties, and immediately sent troops to attack Qi.
Relying on the guidance of heaven and the divine power of the late king, the armies of Zhao and Wei in the area north of the Yellow River all arrived on the shore of Jishui along with the late king.
The troops on the shore of Jishui accepted the order to attack the Qi army and defeated the Qi people.
Our brisk elite troops marched straight into the capital of Qi.
The King of Qi fled to Juyi alone, and he was the only one who escaped death. All the jewels, jade, chariots, armor, and precious sacrificial artifacts were seized and sent back to the State of Yan.
The sacrificial utensils of Qi State are displayed in Ningtai, and the Dalu Bell is displayed in Yuanying Hall; the original treasure tripod of Yan State that was plundered by Qi State was taken from Qi State and returned to the Qing Room. Among the plants in Jiqiu are bamboos transplanted from Wenshui of Qi State. Since the Five Hegemons, their achievements have not caught up with those of the previous kings.
The late king thought that his ambition had been fulfilled, so he set aside a place to reward me so that I could compare with the princes of small countries.
I have heard that a wise and wise monarch builds his achievements without neglecting them, so he can be written in history books such as the Spring and Autumn Annals; a wise man with far-sightedness will not let his reputation deteriorate after he has gained it, so he can be praised by future generations.
Revenge like the previous king did, pacify a powerful country with tens of thousands of chariots, seize the precious treasures accumulated by the Qi Kingdom for more than 800 years, and wait until the day of the death of the previous king to leave political instructions and instructions to ministers in power, revise laws, treat the children of the common people with caution, and extend the kindness to the common people. These can be used to teach future generations.
I have heard this saying that those who are good at pioneering may not necessarily be good at maintaining success, and those who are good at beginning may not necessarily have a good ending.
Once upon a time, Wu Zixu's idea was adopted by King Helu of Wu, who led troops to attack the capital of Chu State Ying. King Wu Fuchai did not accept Wu Zixu's correct advice, but gave him a horse-leather bag to force him to commit suicide, put his bones in the bag and threw him to the river.
King Wu Fuchai didn't understand that Wu Zixu's previous ideas could build merit, so he sank Wu Zixu into the river without regretting it. Wu Zixu also couldn't foresee that monarchs had different temperaments and ambitions, so he was sunk into the river and died with his eyes open.
It is my best policy to avoid being killed and to build meritorious deeds so that the achievements of the late kings can be highlighted.
Being insulted or even slandered, and ruining the reputation of the late king, this is what I fear most.
Facing an unpredictable crime, taking the chance of being spared from death as an opportunity for personal gain is something that a person who abides by morality would not dare to do.
I have heard that gentlemen in ancient times did not speak ill of others when they cut off contact with others; loyal ministers left their original country and did not absolve themselves of their sins and grievances.
Although I have no talent, I have listened to your teachings many times.
I am afraid that you believe the slanderous words of your servant and cannot understand the reason for my alienation from you. So I dedicate this letter to tell you my feelings. I hope the king will pay attention.
So King Hui of Yan named Le Yi's son Le Jian the King of Chang State. Le Yi traveled between Zhao and Yan, and reestablished good relations with Yan. Both Yan and Zhao appointed him as a guest minister.
Le Yi died in the state of Zhao.
Lejian lived in the State of Yan for more than thirty years. The King of Yan liked to adopt the strategy of his prime minister Li Fu and planned to attack the State of Zhao, so he asked Lord Lejian of the State of Chang.
Le Jian said: Zhao is a country at war with all sides. Its people are familiar with military affairs, so it is impossible to attack it.
King Yan didn't like it, so he attacked Zhao.
Lianpo of Zhao State fought back against the Yan army, defeated Lifu's army in Hadi, and captured Lifu and Lecheng.
Lecheng and Lejian have the same ancestor.
So Lejian fled to the State of Zhao, and the State of Zhao attacked the State of Yan.
Yan State ceded a lot of land to Zhao State to seek peace. Only then did Zhao Jun relieve the siege and leave.
King Yan regretted not listening to Yong Lejian's advice. Lejian was already in the state of Zhao, so he wrote a letter to Lejian saying: During the reign of King Zhou of Yin, Jizi was not appointed, but he dared to offend the king, spoke out and remonstrated without slacking off, hoping that King Zhou would listen; Shang Rong was demoted for admonishing King Zhou. He was humiliated, but he still hoped that King Zhou would change his ways.
When the people's morale was weakened, the prisoners escaped one after another, and the country was beyond hope, then the two gentlemen resigned and lived in seclusion.
Therefore, King Zhou had a bad reputation for being violent, but the two gentlemen did not lose their reputation as loyal and noble.
Why is this?
He did his best to worry about the king and the country.
Although I am stupid now, I am not as violent as Yin Zhou; although the people of Yan State are unstable, they are not as serious as the people of Yin Dynasty.
There is a saying that when there is a dispute within a family, one should not express one's own opinions, but tell one's neighbors.
I think these two approaches are undesirable.
Lejian and Lecheng resented the King of Yan for not following their plan, so they finally stayed in Zhao.
Zhao Guo granted Lecheng the title of Lord Wuxiang.
The next year, Lecheng and Lian Po besieged the Yan State for the Zhao State. The Yan State asked Zhao for peace with generous gifts, and the Zhao army began to besiege the Yan State.
Five years later, King Zhao Xiaocheng died.
King Mouxiang sent Lecheng to replace Lian Po's official position.
Lian Po attacked Le Cheng, but Le Cheng fled, and Lian Po also fled to Wei.
Sixteen years later, Qin destroyed Zhao.
More than twenty years later, Emperor Gao of the Han Dynasty passed by the original territory of Zhao and asked the people there: "Does Le Yi have any descendants?"
He replied: There is Uncle Le.
Emperor Gao of the Han Dynasty gave him the title of Yueqing, and he was called Huachengjun.
Hua Chengjun is Le Yi's grandson.
The Le family also included Duke Le Xia and Duke Le Chen. They fled to Qi Gaomi when Zhao was about to be destroyed by Qin.
Duke Lechen was good at studying the theories of Huangdi and Laozi. He was very famous in Qi State, and people called him a wise teacher.
Tai Shigong said: When Kuai Tong and his father-in-law Yan read Le Yi's letter to King Yan, they couldn't help but shed tears when they put down the letter.
Lechengong studied the theories of Huangdi and Laozi. His master was called Heshang Zhangren. It is still unclear where Heshang Zhangren was from.
Heshang's father-in-law taught An Qisheng, An Qisheng taught Duke Mao Xi, Duke Mao Xi taught Duke Lexia, and Duke Lexia taught Duke Lechen. Le Chen taught Gai Gong.
Gaigong taught in Gaomi and Jiaoxi areas of Qidi and was Cao Xiangguo's teacher.
summary:
Le Yi, a descendant of Le Yang who served Wei Wenhou and captured Zhongshan, was a virtuous man fond of military affairs. After King Wuling of Zhao died, Le Yi left Zhao for Wei. Hearing that King Yan Zhao sought revenge against Qi, Le Yi went to Yan as an envoy and became a minister. King Min of Qi grew arrogant and powerful, prompting King Yan Zhao to consult Le Yi about attacking Qi. Le Yi advised forming an alliance with Zhao, Chu, and Wei. King Yan Zhao sent Le Yi to forge alliances, and the allied forces defeated Qi at Jishui. The other states withdrew, but Le Yi’s Yan army pursued Qi to Linzi, capturing treasures and ritual objects. King Yan Zhao granted Le Yi the title of King of Chang Kingdom. Over five years, Le Yi captured over 70 Qi cities, leaving only Ju and Jimo unconquered.

After King Yan Zhao died, King Yan Hui, who resented Le Yi, fell for Qi’s counterintelligence and replaced Le Yi with Qi Jie. Fearing execution, Le Yi surrendered to Zhao, which granted him Guanjin. Qi’s Tian Dan defeated Qi Jie, recovering all Qi cities. King Yan Hui regretted his actions and blamed Le Yi for defecting. Le Yi replied with a letter explaining his loyalty to the late king, his strategic advice, and his fear of unjust punishment. He compared himself to Wu Zixu, emphasizing the importance of preserving the late king’s achievements. King Yan Hui later appointed Le Yi’s son Le Jian as King of Chang State. Le Yi traveled between Zhao and Yan, dying in Zhao.

Le Jian advised against attacking Zhao but was ignored. Zhao’s Lianpo defeated Yan, capturing Lifu and Lecheng. Le Jian fled to Zhao. King Yan regretted his decision. Later, Lecheng and Lian Po besieged Yan, forcing Yan to sue for peace. After King Zhao Xiaocheng died, Lecheng replaced Lian Po, but both fled. Qin eventually destroyed Zhao. Han Gaozu later granted Le Yi’s grandson, Uncle Le, the title of Huachengjun. The Le family also included Duke Le Xia and Duke Le Chen, who fled to Qi Gaomi. Duke Le Chen studied Huangdi and Laozi, becoming a renowned teacher. Sima Qian noted that reading Le Yi’s letter moved him to tears.
