translation:
Although there are many books that scholars have read, they still need to examine the authentic and trustworthy records in the Six Classics.
Although the "Book of Songs" and "Shangshu" are incomplete, it can still be clearly examined from the texts recording the Yu and Xia dynasties.
When Tang Yao was about to abdicate, he gave up the throne to Yu Shun. When Yu Shun gave up the throne to Xia Yu, princes from all over the world and the state pastors came to recommend him, and then he was placed in the position of emperor for inspection and trial. After presiding over the state affairs for decades and making outstanding achievements, the government was handed over to him.
This means that the world is a very precious treasure, and the emperor is a very important leader, so the power is so solemn and prudent!
However, it is said in Zhuzi's Miscellaneous Notes: Tang Yao wanted to cede the world to Xu You, but Xu You not only refused to accept it, but took it as a shame, so he ran away and lived in seclusion.
In the Xia Dynasty, Bian Sui and Wuguang appeared again who did not accept Shang Tang's abdication.
How does this glorify them?
Tai Shigong said: I climbed Jishan Mountain and said that there might be Xu You's tomb on the mountain.
Confucius discussed in turn the benevolent people, saints, and wise men in ancient times, such as Wu Taibo and Boyi, all in great detail.
I think the virtues of Xu You and Wu Guang that I have heard are the noblest, but there is not even a rough written record in the scriptures. Why is this?
Confucius said: Boyi and Shuqi do not remember their past hatred, so their resentment will be less.
They pursue benevolence and get it, so why do they have any resentment?
I deeply sympathized with Boyi's will, and I was surprised to see their poems that were not recorded in the scriptures.
Their biographies say: Boyi and Shuqi are the two sons of Lord Guzhu.
His father wanted to make Shuqi the king of the country. When his father died, Shuqi wanted to give up the throne to Boyi.
Boyi said: This is my father's last wish!
So he ran away.
Shu Qi also refused to inherit the throne and ran away.
The people of the country had no choice but to support Lord Guzhu's second son.
At this time, Boyi and Shuqi heard that Xibochang could provide good support to the elderly, so they thought why not join him!
But when they got there, Xibochang was already dead. His son, King Wu, honored Xibochang as King Wen, carried his wooden spiritual tablet on a chariot, and marched eastward to attack Yin Zhou.
Boyi and Shuqi reined in King Wu's horse and remonstrated, saying: "If my father dies without being buried, then he starts a war. Can it be called filial piety?"
Can it be said that killing a monarch as a minister is benevolent and righteous?
The entourage around King Wu wanted to kill them.
Taigong Lu Shang said: This is a man of integrity.
So he helped them leave.
When King Wu put down the rebellion of Shang Zhou, the whole world surrendered to the Zhou Dynasty, but Boyi and Shuqi thought it was a shame. They insisted on benevolence and righteousness, refused to eat the food of the Zhou Dynasty, and lived in seclusion on Shouyang Mountain, picking wild vegetables to satisfy their hunger.
When I was about to starve to death, I composed a song with the words: Climb up the Western Mountain and pick the weeds there.
Exchange a tyrant minister for a tyrant, but you don't realize that it is a mistake.
The peaceful and prosperous times of Shennong, Yu and Xia disappeared in a blink of an eye. Where is our destination?
Alas, there is only death, such a bad fate!
So he starved to death in Shouyang Mountain.
From this poem, do they feel resentful or not?
Some people say: Heaven is impartial and always helps good people.
Should Boyi and Shuqi be said to be good people, or should they not be said to be good people?
They accumulated benevolence and maintained noble conduct, but they finally starved to death!
Besides, among Confucius's seventy proud students, only Yan Yuan was praised as a studious scholar. However, Yan Yuan was always poor and could not even eat poor food, and finally died prematurely.
What is the reward of heaven for good people?
Robber Zhi killed innocent people all day long, roasted people's hearts and livers and ate them as meat. He was cruel and indulgent, gathered thousands of gang members and ran rampant in the world, and he died a long life.
What kind of ethics does this follow?
This is a great and remarkable thing.
As for modern times, those who do not follow the right path and specifically break laws and regulations can live a life of ease and enjoyment, and live a wealthy and generous life for generations to come.
But there are people who are willing to walk only when they choose a good place, who are willing to talk or walk only when the opportunity is right, and dare not go through small roads. They will never do anything that is not just and vigorous. There are countless people who are cautious and suffer disaster like this.
I was deeply confused. If there is such a thing as the way of heaven, then is this the way of heaven, or not the way of heaven?
Confucius said: People who have different ideas cannot discuss with each other. Only each person acts according to his own will.
So he said: If wealth can be sought, I will do it even if I am a humble driver; if I cannot find it, I will still do it according to my own hobbies.  In the severe cold season, we realize that the pines and cypresses are the last to wither.
When the whole society is chaotic and dirty, people with noble character will emerge.
Isn't it because some people take wealth and happiness so seriously that other people take wealth and happiness so lightly?
Confucius said: What a gentleman fears is not having his name mentioned until his death.
Jia Yi said: Those who are greedy for money die for money, those who value righteousness over life sacrifice themselves for fame, those who are boastful and greedy for power die fighting for power, and ordinary people are greedy for life and hate death.
The Book of Changes says: Things that are equally bright will reflect each other, and things that belong to the same category will naturally react to each other.
The colorful clouds soar with the dragon's roar, the valley wind rises with the roar of the tiger, and the sage's description reveals the true nature of all things.
Although Boyi and Shuqi were virtuous, their reputations only became more prominent when they were praised by Confucius.
Yan Yuan was dedicated to learning, and it was only because he followed Confucius that his virtues became more remarkable.
The hermits in the caves of Yanju are either well-known or unknown. This is sometimes the case. What a pity it would be for a person like this to have a buried reputation and not be praised!
Scholars in remote areas must cultivate their virtues and establish a reputation. If they don't rely on people who are Delong and Wangzun, how can they be famous for future generations?
summary:
The text examines the authenticity of historical records, emphasizing the need to rely on trustworthy sources like the Six Classics, despite the incomplete state of the "Book of Songs" and "Shangshu." It recounts the abdication of Tang Yao to Yu Shun and Yu Shun to Xia Yu, highlighting the solemn and prudent transfer of power, as the world and emperor are precious. However, it contrasts this with accounts from Zhuzi's Miscellaneous Notes, where Xu You refused Tang Yao's abdication out of shame, and later Bian Sui and Wuguang similarly rejected Shang Tang's offer. The historian climbs Jishan Mountain, noting Xu You's possible tomb, and reflects on Confucius's discussions of ancient sages like Wu Taibo and Boyi, yet finds no scriptural record of Xu You and Wu Guang's virtues.

The text then focuses on Boyi and Shuqi, sons of Lord Guzhu, who fled to avoid a succession dispute. They sought Xibochang but found him dead; his son King Wu marched against Yin Zhou. Boyi and Shuqi remonstrated against this, citing filial piety and righteousness, but were spared by Taigong Lu Shang. After the Zhou conquest, they refused to eat Zhou's food, starved on Shouyang Mountain, and composed a song expressing despair. The historian questions whether they felt resentment, given their fate.

It further questions divine justice: Boyi and Shuqi, though virtuous, starved, while Yan Yuan, a studious disciple, died poor. In contrast, the cruel Robber Zhi lived long. Modern times show lawbreakers thriving while the righteous suffer. The historian is perplexed by this apparent lack of heavenly reward. Confucius's sayings on differing paths and the value of virtue in chaotic times are cited. The text concludes that reputation depends on association with the virtuous, as Boyi and Shuqi were glorified by Confucius, and scholars in obscurity need such recognition to be remembered.

translation:
As the saying goes: If you work hard, it is better to have a good year; if you are good at being an official, it is better to have good luck in officialdom.
This is not empty talk without basis. Not only did women use their beauty to flatter themselves to gain favor, but this also happened among scholars and eunuchs.
In the past, many people used their beauty to gain favor.
By the time the Han Dynasty was founded, Emperor Gaozu was extremely violent and upright, but he had children who were favored by flattery. During the reign of Emperor Xiaohui, there was a Hong child who was like this.
These two people had no talent, but gained the favor of the nobles through their gentleness and flattery. They actually slept together with the emperor, and even the ministers had to go through them before they could make presentations to the emperor.
Therefore, during the period of Emperor Xiaohui, the ministers and ministers all wore hats decorated with bird feathers, belts decorated with shells, and applied makeup and powder. This was the result of the influence of Hongru and Jiru.
Later, Hong Ru and Ji Ru both moved their families to Anling.
During the reign of Emperor Xiaowen, the favored ministers in the palace included Deng Tong as a scholar and Zhao Tong and Beigong Bozi as eunuchs.
Beigong Bozi was favored because he was a kind elder; Zhao Tong was favored because he was good at observing stars and fortune telling, and often served as Emperor Wen's companion; Deng Tong had no skills.
Deng Tong was from Nan'an, Shu County. He became a Huangtou Lang because he was good at rowing.
Emperor Xiaowen dreamed of ascending to heaven, but he couldn't go up. A yellow-headed man pushed him up to heaven from behind. He looked back and saw the man's clothes at the waist, and his belt was knotted behind his back.
After waking up from the dream, Emperor Wen went to Jiantai and secretly looked for Huang Toulang who pushed him to heaven based on what he saw in his dream. Sure enough, he saw Deng Tong, his belt tied behind his back, just as he had seen in his dream.
Emperor Wen summoned him and asked his name, and he replied that his surname was Deng Mingtong. Emperor Wen liked him and respected and favored him more and more day by day.
Deng Tong was also honest and cautious, and did not like to interact with outsiders. Although the emperor granted him a vacation, he did not want to go out.
At that time, the emperor rewarded him more than ten times, with a total of hundreds of millions of money, and his official position was promoted to senior doctor.
Emperor Wen often visited Deng Tong's house to play and play.
But Deng Tong had no other talents and could not recommend wise men. He was just cautious in doing things and trying to please the emperor.
Once, the emperor asked a person who was good at fortune telling to look at Deng Tong. After the person looked at him, he said: Deng Tong will die of poverty and hunger.
Emperor Wen said: I am the one who can make Deng Tong rich. How can I say that he will be poor?
So Emperor Wen gave Deng Tong the Tongshan in Yandao, Shu County, and allowed him to mint his own money. From then on, "Deng's money" spread throughout the country.
His wealth reached this level.
Emperor Wen once suffered from carbuncle, and Deng usually sucked the pus and blood for him.
Emperor Wen was unhappy and asked Deng Tong calmly: "Who in the world loves me the most?"
Deng Tong said: There should be no one who loves you more than the prince.
The prince came to see Emperor Wen's condition, and Emperor Wen asked him to suck the pus and blood. Although the prince sucked the pus and blood, his face showed embarrassment.
Later, when the prince heard that Deng Tong sucked pus and blood for Emperor Wen, he felt ashamed and resented Deng Tong.
When Emperor Wen died and Emperor Jing came to the throne, Deng Tong was dismissed and lived at home.
Soon after, someone reported that Deng Tong had stolen coins minted from abroad.
Emperor Jing brought the matter to the judge, and it turned out that it was true, so he closed the case and confiscated all the Deng Tong family's money, leaving hundreds of millions in debt.
The eldest princess Liu gave Deng Tong money as a prostitute, but the officials immediately confiscated it and took it back to the palace. Deng Tong was not even allowed to wear a hairpin.
So the eldest princess ordered her subordinates to lend Deng Tong only the cost of food and clothing.
After all, Deng Tong was not allowed to own a penny and lived in other people's homes until his death.
During the reign of Emperor Xiaojing, there were no favored ministers in the palace, but only the Langzhong Ling Zhou Ren. He was the most favored, more favored than ordinary people, but still not deep.
Today, the favored ministers and scholars in the palace of Emperor Wu of Han include Han Yan, the great-grandson of King Han, and the eunuch is Li Yannian.
Han Yan is the concubine and grandson of Gong Gaohou Han Daidang.
When the emperor was the king of Jiaodong, Han Yan and the emperor were calligraphy classmates and became friendly with each other.
When the emperor became the prince, he became closer to Han Yan.
Yan is good at riding horses and archery, and is good at flattery.
When the emperor came to the throne, he wanted to attack the Huns, so Han Yan learned the weapons and formations of the Hu people in advance. For this reason, he became more and more distinguished, and his official position was promoted to a senior official. The emperor's rewards were comparable to those of Deng Tong.
At that time, Han Yan often slept and woke up with the emperor.
Once, Liu Fei, the king of Jiangdu, went to Beijing to see Emperor Wu. He was ordered to follow the emperor to Shanglin Garden for hunting.
The emperor's chariot had not yet set off due to the clearing of the road, so he sent Han Yan to take the auxiliary chariot, followed by hundreds of cavalry, and galloped forward to observe the whereabouts of the wild beasts.
King Jiangdu saw it from a distance and thought it was the emperor coming, so he asked his followers to stay away and he lay down by the roadside to pay his respects.
However, Han Yan galloped past on horseback and did not see King Jiangdu.
After Han Yan passed by, King Jiangdu was furious and cried to the Queen Mother: Please allow me to return the feudal state to the court and return to the palace as a guard on duty, just like Han Yan.
The Queen Mother resented Han Yan.
Han Yan served the emperor and was not prohibited from entering or leaving the residence of the concubines in Yongxiang. Someone reported the adultery to the empress dowager.
The Empress Dowager was furious and sent an envoy to order Han Yan to commit suicide.
Emperor Wu apologized and begged the Queen Mother on his behalf, but was ultimately not accepted and Han Yan committed suicide.
Case Road Hou Han said that he was his younger brother, and he was favored because of his flattery.
Li Yannian was from the Zhongshan Kingdom. His parents, him and his brothers and sisters were all singing and dancing artists.
Li Yannian was castrated for breaking the law and then served in the Dog Prison.
Princess Pingyang, the sister of Emperor Wu, told the emperor that Li Yannian's sister was good at dancing. The emperor summoned her and he liked her very much. After Li Yannian's sister was summoned to the palace, he also summoned Li Yannian to the palace to make him noble.
Li Yannian was good at singing and composed new music. At this time, the emperor was building the Tiandi Temple and wanted to compose lyrics to accompany the music.
Li Yannian was good at catering to the emperor's wishes and sang the new music to match the music.
His sister was also favored by Emperor Wu and gave birth to a son.
Li Yannian wore the official seal of two thousand stones, which was called "Xie Sheng Lv".
He slept and rose with the emperor, and was very noble and favored, similar to the favor that Han Yan received.
After a long time, Li Yannian gradually began to engage in lewd behavior with the palace maids, going in and out of the palace, becoming arrogant and indulgent.
After the death of his sister, Mrs. Li, the emperor's favor with him waned, and eventually Li Yannian and his brothers were arrested and killed.
From then on, most of the courtiers favored by the emperor in the palace were relatives of foreign relatives, but these people are not worth talking about.
Wei Qing and Huo Qubing were also favored by dignitaries because of their relatives, but they were able to make contributions based on their own talents.
Tai Shigong said: The timing of the emperor's favor and hatred is terrible!
From Mi Zixia's experience, we can completely see the ending of the ungrateful people in future generations.
It can be known even after EMI.
summary:
The text discusses how favoritism and flattery, rather than talent, often led to power and wealth in ancient China, particularly during the Han Dynasty. It begins by noting that both women and men, including scholars and eunuchs, used beauty and charm to gain imperial favor. During Emperor Gaozu's reign, flattery was common, and under Emperor Xiaohui, two favored individuals, Hongru and Jiru, gained such influence that ministers imitated their dress and behavior. They later moved to Anling.

Under Emperor Xiaowen, favored figures included Deng Tong, a boatman who became a high-ranking official after the emperor dreamed of a yellow-headed man pushing him to heaven, which matched Deng Tong's appearance. Deng Tong was rewarded generously, given a copper mountain to mint his own money, and even sucked pus from the emperor's carbuncle. However, after Emperor Jing ascended, Deng Tong was accused of minting coins abroad, his wealth was confiscated, and he died in poverty.

During Emperor Xiaojing's reign, only Zhou Ren was favored, but not deeply. Under Emperor Wu, Han Yan, a calligraphy classmate, gained favor through flattery and military skills, often sleeping with the emperor. He was later accused of adultery with concubines and forced to commit suicide by the empress dowager. Li Yannian, a singer and dancer, rose to favor after his sister became Emperor Wu's concubine, but after her death, his arrogance led to his execution.

The text concludes that most favored courtiers were relatives of the emperor, though Wei Qing and Huo Qubing achieved merit through talent. Tai Shigong reflects on the unpredictability of imperial favor, citing Mi Zixia as an example of ingratitude.

translation:
Fu Kuan, the Marquis of Yangling, followed Pei Gong Liu Bang as a cavalry officer with the title of the fifth official of Wei State. He once served as a retainer and started an uprising in Hengyang.
He followed Pei Gong to attack Anyang and Gangli, attacked Qin general Zhao Bi's army in Kaifeng, and defeated Qin general Yang Xiong's army in Quyu and Yangwu, capturing twelve enemy heads. Pei Gong granted him the title of Qing.
Later he followed Pei Gong to Bashang.
Peigong became the queen of Han Dynasty and gave Fu Kuan the title of Gongdejun.
He followed the King of Han into the Hanzhong area and was promoted to the right cavalry general.
Soon after, he followed the King of Han to pacify the Three Qin Dynasties, and the King of Han gave Diaoyin County as his seat.
During the conflict between Chu and Han, he followed the King of Han to attack Xiang Yu, the overlord of Western Chu, and was ordered to meet the King of Han in Huai County. The King of Han gave him the title of Marquis of Tongde.
When he followed the King of Han to attack Xiang Yu's generals Xiang Guan, Zhou Lan, and Long Qie, the soldiers he led captured an enemy cavalry general at the foot of Aocang Mountain, thus increasing his food supply.
Fu Kuan was once under the command of Huaiyin Marquis Han Xin, and defeated Qi's garrison at Lixia and defeated Qi's general Tian Jie.
Later, it came under the command of Cao Shen, the prime minister, and conquered Bo County of Qi State, adding more food towns.
Because of his meritorious service in pacifying Qi, the king of Han divided the token in half and gave him half to show his credibility, so that his title could be passed down from generation to generation. He was granted the title of Marquis of Yangling, with a settlement of 2,600 households, and he was granted the title of a settlement before.
Later, he served as the right prime minister of Qi State and stationed troops to guard against Tian Heng's rebellion.
He served as prime minister of Qi for five years.
In April of the eleventh year of Emperor Gaozu of the Han Dynasty, he attacked Chen Xi, who rebelled against the Han and proclaimed himself king. He was under the command of Taiwei Zhou Bo, who replaced Fan Kuai, the prime minister of the Han Dynasty, and defeated Chen Xi.
In January of the next year, he was transferred to Dai Guo Xiangguo and led troops to garrison the border counties.
Two years later, he served as the prime minister of the country and continued to lead troops to garrison the border counties.
Fu Kuan died in the fifth year of Emperor Hui of the Han Dynasty, and his posthumous title was Jinghou.
His son Qinghou Fu Jing inherited the title, and his son Qinghou died after reigning for twenty-four years.
Fu Jing's son, Gonghou Fu Ze, inherited the title and died after reigning for twelve years.
Fu Ze's son Fu Yan inherited the title. In the thirty-first year, he was executed for treason with Liu An, King of Huainan, and his fiefdom was abolished.
Jin She, the Marquis of Xinwu, followed Pei Gong Liu Bang as an attendant official and raised troops in Wanqu.
He once attacked Jiyang.
Defeated the army of Qin general Li You.
He also attacked the Qin army in the south of Bo County and northeast of Kaifeng. He killed one general with a thousand cavalry, captured fifty-seven heads, and captured seventy-three people. He received the title given by Pei Gong and was named Lord Linping.
Later, they fought in the north of Lantian, killing two Qin army chariot commanders and one cavalry officer, beheading 28 heads and capturing 57 people.
Then he led his army to Bashang.
At that time, Peigong was the king of Han Dynasty and granted Jin She the title of Marquis of Jianwu and promoted him to the rank of Cavalry Commander.
Jin She followed the King of Han and pacified the Three Qin Dynasties.
In addition, he led his troops to march westward to attack Qin General Zhang Ping's army in Longxi, defeated the Qin army, and pacified six counties in Longxi. The soldiers he led killed four Qin army chariot commanders, four military commanders, and twelve cavalry officers.
Later, he followed the King of Han to attack the Chu army eastward and reached Pengcheng.
As a result, the Han army was defeated. Jin She defended Yongqiu, and then left Yongqiu to attack Wang Wu and others who rebelled against the Han.
After seizing Liang, he led his troops to attack the Chu general Xing Shuo's army stationed in Shannan, and defeated Xing Shuo. He personally captured Xing Shuo's two captains, 12 Sima and junhou, and surrendered 4,180 enemy officers and soldiers.
In addition, the Chu army was defeated in the east of Xingyang.
In the third year of the reign of Emperor Gaozu of the Han Dynasty, Jin She was given a city of 4,200 households.
Jin She also led his troops to Hanoi and attacked the Zhao general Ben Hao who was stationed in Chaoge. He was defeated. The soldiers he led captured the two generals alive and captured 250 horses.
He followed the King of Han to attack the area east of Anyang, reaching Jipu and capturing seven counties.
He also led another troops to defeat Zhao's army, captured alive Zhao's two Sima soldiers and four military commanders, and recruited 2,400 Zhao officers and soldiers to surrender.
He also followed the King of Han to conquer Handan.
He led his troops to capture Pingyang alone, and personally beheaded Zhao's acting prime minister who was stationed in Pingyang. The soldiers he led beheaded the leading county guard and the county guard, forcing Ye to surrender.
In this campaign, he followed the Han king to attack Chaoge and Handan, and defeated the Zhao army separately, forcing the six counties of Handan County to surrender.
After leading his army back to Aocang, he immediately defeated Xiang Yu's army in Chenggaonan, destroyed and cut off the channel transporting grain and wages from Xingyang to Xiangyi.
They defeated the Xiang champion team at the foot of Lucheng and captured a large area of ​​land stretching from Zeng, Tan and Xiapi in the east to Qi and Zhuyi in the south.
He also defeated Xiang Han's army at the foot of Jiyang City.
Then he sent his army back to attack Xiang Yu's troops under Chen County City, and defeated Xiang Yu.
In addition, Jiangling was pacified, and the king of Linjiang's Zhu Guo, Da Sima and eight of his subordinates who were in Jiangling were surrendered. He personally captured Linjiang Wang's common lieutenant alive and escorted him to Luoyang, thus pacifying Nanjun.
After that, he followed the King of Han to Chen County and arrested the evil Chu King Han Xin. The King of Han divided the talisman representing the voucher into two halves and gave one half to Jin She to show his credibility, so that his title could be passed down from generation to generation. He provided for 4,600 households in the settlement and was titled Xin Wuhou.
Later, Jin She followed Emperor Gao as a cavalry captain to attack Dai Wang, defeated Dai Wang Han Xin at the foot of Pingcheng, and then led his army back to Dongyuan.
Because of his meritorious service, he was promoted to general of chariots and cavalry, and then led the troops of Liang, Zhao, Qi, Yan, and Chu princes to attack Chen Xi's prime minister Hou Chang in separate groups, defeating him and forcing Qu Nicheng to surrender.
Later, he followed Emperor Gaozu and made great achievements in attacking Tingbu. He was granted an additional 5,300 households with stipulated food settlements.
In several important battles, Jin She beheaded 90 enemies and captured 132 people. He also defeated the enemy 14 times, surrendered 59 cities, pacified one county and one country, and 23 counties. He captured alive one person each from the princes, kings and Zhuguo, and 39 officials of different ranks ranging from less than 2,000 shi to 500 shi.
In the fifth year of his reign, Jin She passed away and was given the posthumous title Suhou.
His son Jin Ting inherited the title of Marquis.
Twenty-one years later, in the third year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Han Dynasty, he was deprived of his title and exempted from the fiefdom because he drove the people beyond the regulations.
Kuai Chenghou, named Gui, was from Peixian County and his surname was Zhou.
He once served as Gaozu's bodyguard and followed Gaozu in rebellion as a retainer.
He once accompanied Emperor Gaozu to Bashang, and then went west to enter the Shu and Han regions. Later, he returned with Emperor Gaozu and pacified the Three Qin Dynasties, and was granted the title of Chiyang as a food town.
He was ordered to lead his troops eastward to cut off the enemy's transportation channels. Then he followed Gaozu on his expedition and crossed Pingyin Ferry to the east, meeting up with the troops of Huaiyin Marquis Han Xin in Xiangguo. At that time, the battles were sometimes won and sometimes defeated, and the situation was grim, but Zhou Ji never meant to deviate from Gaozu.
Emperor Gaozu granted him the title of Marquis of Xinwu, with a settlement of 3,300 households.
In the twelfth year of Emperor Gaozu's reign, Zhou Gui was granted the title of Marquis of Kuai Cheng, and at the same time, he was exempted from the original food fief.
Emperor Gaozu once wanted to attack Chen Xi personally, but Marquis Kuai Cheng tried to dissuade him with tears: In the past, the King of Qin conquered the world without going out in person. Now you often go out in person. Doesn't this mean that you have no more people to dispatch?
Emperor Gao believed that Zhou Xie sincerely cared for him, and made an exception to allow him to enter the palace without having to walk quickly. He would not be punished with death if he killed someone.
In the fifth year of Emperor Wen of the Han Dynasty, Zhou Hui died of old age and was given the posthumous title Zhenhou.
His son Zhou Chang inherited the title of marquis, but was exempted from the fiefdom due to a crime.
In the second year of the Zhongyuan period of Emperor Jing of the Han Dynasty, Zhou Ju, the son of Zhou Jie, was granted the title of Marquis on his behalf.
In the third year of Yuanding, Emperor Wu of the Han Dynasty, Zhou Ju was appointed as Taichang official. He committed a crime and was exempted from his fiefdom.
Tai Shigong said: Fu Kuan, the Marquis of Yangling, and Jin She, the Marquis of Xinwu, both received high titles. They followed Emperor Gaozu and raised troops from Shandong, attacked Xiang Yu, killed famous generals, defeated dozens of enemy armies, and surrendered dozens of cities. They did not suffer setbacks or hardships. This is also a gift from God.
The Marquis of Kuai Cheng and Zhou Jie were staunch and loyal, and were never doubted by anyone. Every time Gaozu went on an expedition, he never failed to shed tears. This can only be done by a person with a very painful heart. He can be said to be a loyal and kind gentleman.
summary:
The text summarizes the military careers and titles of three Han Dynasty figures: Fu Kuan, Jin She, and Zhou Xie (Kuai Chenghou), as recorded in historical records.

Fu Kuan, Marquis of Yangling, began as a cavalry officer under Pei Gong (Liu Bang). He participated in numerous battles against Qin and Chu forces, including attacks on Anyang, Gangli, and Kaifeng, and defeating generals like Zhao Bi and Yang Xiong. After Liu Bang became King of Han, Fu Kuan was promoted to right cavalry general and granted Diaoyin County. He fought against Xiang Yu, capturing an enemy cavalry general at Aocang Mountain. Serving under Han Xin and later Cao Shen, he helped pacify Qi, earning a hereditary title as Marquis of Yangling with 2,600 households. He served as right prime minister of Qi for five years, guarded against Tian Heng’s rebellion, and attacked Chen Xi under Zhou Bo. Fu Kuan died in the fifth year of Emperor Hui’s reign, posthumously named Jinghou. His lineage continued until his descendant Fu Yan was executed for treason with Liu An, King of Huainan.

Jin She, Marquis of Xinwu, served as an attendant under Pei Gong, raising troops in Wanqu. He defeated Qin forces under Li You and others, capturing numerous enemies and receiving the title Linping Lord. After Liu Bang became King of Han, Jin She was made Marquis of Jianwu and Cavalry Commander. He pacified the Three Qin Dynasties, defeated Zhang Ping in Longxi, and fought in the Chu-Han conflict, capturing Xing Shuo’s officers and surrendering 4,180 troops. He conquered Zhao forces, captured Pingyang, and forced Ye to surrender. He defeated Xiang Yu’s army at Chenggao and Lucheng, pacified Nanjun, and arrested Han Xin. He was titled Xinwuhou with 4,600 households, later increased to 5,300. Jin She died in the fifth year of his reign, posthumously named Suhou. His son Jin Ting inherited but lost the title in the third year of Emperor Wen’s reign.

Zhou Xie, Marquis of Kuai Cheng, from Peixian, served as Gaozu’s bodyguard and retainer. He accompanied Gaozu to Bashang, entered Shu and Han, and pacified the Three Qin Dynasties, receiving Chiyang as a fief. He led troops eastward to cut enemy supply lines and joined Han Xin at Xiangguo. Gaozu granted him the title of Marquis of Xinwu with 3,300 households, later changed to Kuai Chenghou. He once tearfully dissuaded Gaozu from personally attacking Chen Xi, earning special privileges. Zhou Xie died in the fifth year of Emperor Wen’s reign, posthumously named Zhenhou. His son Zhou Chang inherited but lost the title due to crime. Zhou Ju, his grandson, was later granted the title but lost it in the third year of Emperor Wu’s reign.

Sima Qian comments that Fu Kuan and Jin She received high titles, raised troops from Shandong, defeated dozens of enemy armies, and surrendered many cities without setbacks, attributing this to divine favor. Zhou Xie, loyal and unwavering, never doubted by Gaozu, wept during expeditions, showing a deeply loyal and kind heart.
rel_path: 史记/七十列传/傅靳蒯成列传

translation:
Tai Shigong said: When I read the regulations for the imperial examination and selection of academic officials, and when I read that Guang Kai encouraged academic officials to start education, I could not help but put down the book and sigh, saying: Alas, the Zhou royal family has declined, and the poem "Guan Ju" that satirizes current affairs has appeared;
Therefore, Confucius was worried about the decline of the royal way and the rise of evil ways, so he compiled "Poems" and "Books" and organized ritual music.
When he went to Qi State and heard the wonderful music of "Shao", he became so addicted that he couldn't taste the delicious meat for three months.
After he returned to Lu from Wei, he began to correct the music so that the songs of "Ya" and "Song" could be put into their proper places and in an orderly manner.
Because the world was chaotic and dirty, no one could use him. Therefore, Confucius traveled around the country and asked more than 70 kings for officials, but he could not get any advice. He said with emotion: If someone is willing to use me, it will only take one year to manage the country's affairs.
Someone hunted unicorns in the western suburbs of the state of Lu. Confucius lamented that his ideals could not be realized after hearing about it. So he wrote the Spring and Autumn Annals with the help of the existing historical records of the state of Lu, and used it to express the emperor's royal laws.
Since the death of Confucius, more than seventy of his good students have dispersed to make friends with the princes. Those who have achieved great success have become teachers and ministers of princes and kings, while those who have achieved little success have made friends and taught scholars and officials, while some have lived in seclusion and are not serving as officials.
Therefore, Zilu served as an official in the state of Wei, Zizhang served as an official in the state of Chen, Ziyu of Tantai lived in the state of Chu, Zixia taught in Xihe, and Zigong lived in Qi until his death.
People like Tian Zifang, Duan Qianmu, Wu Qi, and Qin Huali were all trained by Zixia's generation, and then became teachers of princes and kings.
At that time, only Wei Wenhou was the most humbly seeking advice from Confucianism. Later, Confucianism gradually declined until it suffered annihilation at the hands of Qin Shihuang. During the Warring States Period, when all the great powers in the world were fighting against each other, Confucianism had been squeezed out, but in the Qi and Lu areas, those who studied and studied it never abandoned it.
During the reign of King Wei of Qi and King Xuan of Qi, Mencius, Xunzi and others all inherited Confucius' work and carried it forward, becoming famous in the world with their own theories.
At the end of the Qin Dynasty, Qin Shihuang burned "Poems" and "Books" and killed Confucian scholars. The Six Arts of Confucian classics have been incomplete since then.
Chen She rebelled against Qin and proclaimed himself king. The Confucian scholars in Lu went to him carrying Confucius' ancestral ritual vessels.
So Kong Jia became Chen She's doctor and eventually died with him.
Chen She started out as an ordinary citizen and led a group of rioters to guard the border. Within a month, he became the king of Chu, but within half a year he was destroyed again. His career was very small and shallow, but the scholar-bureaucrats carried Confucius's ritual vessels to follow and submit to him. Why?
Because the Qin Dynasty burned their books and studies, they accumulated grudges, which forced them to seek refuge with King Chen to vent their anger.
Until Emperor Gaozu killed Xiang Ji and led his troops to surround the state of Lu, the Confucian scholars in the state of Lu were still reciting scriptures and practicing rituals and music, and the sound of string songs was endless. Isn't this the legacy of the ancient saints? Isn't this a country that deeply loves rituals and music?
So after Confucius traveled to the state of Chen, he said: Go back!
Go back. The young people in our village have lofty aspirations and brilliant literary talent. I don’t know how to teach them.
Qilu people attach great importance to cultural rituals. This has been the case since ancient times. This has become a natural trend.
After the establishment of the Han Dynasty, Confucian scholars began to have the opportunity to re-study the classics, and they also taught and practiced the etiquette of shooting and drinking.
After Shusun Tong formulated the etiquette of the Han Dynasty, he became a Taichang official. The Confucian disciples who formulated the etiquette with him were also elected as court officials, so people sighed and became interested in Confucianism.
However, at that time, the war in the world had not yet ceased, and the emperor was busy pacifying the world and had no time to consider the establishment of schools.
When Emperor Xiaohui and Empress Lu were in power, the ministers were all highly skilled in martial arts and had outstanding military exploits.
During the reign of Emperor Xiaowen, he briefly appointed Confucian scholars as officials, but Emperor Xiaowen originally only loved the theory of punishment names.
When Emperor Xiaojing came to power, there was no need for Confucian scholars, and Empress Dowager Dou was fond of Taoist thought. Therefore, the doctorate positions were only reserved for officials waiting for imperial edicts.
Until the emperor came to the throne today, Zhao Wan, Wang Zang and others had a deep understanding of Confucianism, and the emperor also yearned for it, so the imperial court ordered the recommendation of scribes and scholars who were virtuous, upright and proficient in Confucian classics.
From then on, those who taught "Shi" included Shen Peigong in Lu, Yuan Yinsheng in Qi, and Han Taifu in Yan.
Among those who talked about Shangshu was Jinan Fusheng.
Among those who talked about "Li" was Gaotangsheng from Lu.
Among those who taught "Spring and Autumn" were Hu Wusheng in Qi and Lu, and Dong Zhongshu in Zhao.
After the death of Empress Dowager Dou, Tian Fu, Marquis of Wu'an, became the prime minister. He abandoned Taoism, Xing Mingjia and other hundreds of schools of thought, and invited hundreds of Confucian scholars to serve as officials in the court. However, Gongsun Hong rose through the ranks due to his proficiency in the "Spring and Autumn".
From then on, students all over the world became fascinated and devoted themselves to studying Confucianism.
Gongsun Hong once worshiped as a doctor. He was afraid that Confucianism would be blocked and not spread. After he became the prime minister, he petitioned: The Prime Minister's Censor reported to the emperor: His Majesty once ordered: I heard that politicians should teach the people with etiquette and inspire them with music.
Marriage is the most important ethical relationship between husband and wife.
Now that rituals and music have been destroyed and abandoned, I am deeply worried.
Therefore, we vigorously invite people with upright moral character and broad knowledge from all over the world to come and serve as officials in the dynasty.
I ordered the ritual officials to study diligently, lecture on Confucianism, be knowledgeable, and revive ritual and music, so as to set an example for the people of the world.
He also ordered Taichang to discuss and allocate disciples and students to doctors, so that the people would respect education and open up the way to cultivate talents.
According to His Majesty’s decree. The ministers, Taichang, Kong Zang, and Doctors discussed and decided seriously: I heard that the way of governing the country in the Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties was that there were places of education in every village. In the Xia dynasty, it was called the school, in the Yin dynasty, it was called the Xu, and in the Zhou dynasty, it was called Xiang.
When they encouraged those who did good, they allowed them to be prominent in the court; when they punished those who did evil, they punished them.
Therefore, the implementation of enlightenment must first set an example in the capital city and then be promoted from the capital city to local areas.
Now His Majesty has made it clear that his supreme kindness is radiating as bright as the sun and the moon. It is in line with the great way of heaven and earth. It is based on regulating human relations, encouraging learning, paying attention to etiquette, advocating education, and rewarding the virtuous. In this way, the four directions in the sea will change to good. This is the basis for realizing the rule of peace!
In ancient times, the political education was unharmonious and the etiquette was incomplete. Now it is requested to use the original official positions to flourish it.
Please assign fifty disciples to the doctorate and exempt them from taxes and corvee.
Let Taichang select people over the age of 18 with good appearance from among the common people to supplement his Ph.D. disciples.
If there are people in prefectures, counties, provinces, and towns who like classics, respect their superiors, strictly abide by politics and religion, are friendly to their neighbors, and do not violate the teachings they have learned when going out or going out, the county magistrate, the princes, the county magistrate, and the county magistrate should recommend them to the superior county governors and princes and kingdoms. Those who pass the careful inspection should go to Taichang in the capital with the superior planners to receive the same education as the doctoral disciples.
They have to take exams after one year of study. Those who can master more than one kind of classics can supplement the vacancies of literary and anecdotal officials. Among them, those with good grades and high rankings can be appointed as doctors, and Taichang will compile a book and record it.
If they are particularly outstanding, their names can be reported directly.
Those whose talents are low if they do not study hard, and those who are not proficient in a certain kind of classics, will be dismissed and the incompetent officials who recommended them will be punished.
I carefully understand the edicts and laws issued by Your Majesty. They clarify the relationship between the way of heaven and the way of humanity, and integrate the principles of governing the country from ancient times to the present. The articles are elegant and upright, and the words of instruction are profound and rich in meaning. Their immeasurable kindness will deeply benefit the people of the country.
However, the petty officials had little knowledge and could not explain the imperial edicts and laws thoroughly, and could not clearly communicate His Majesty's will to the world.
The duties of administering etiquette and telling stories were performed by people who understood the classics and etiquette. Their promotion was very slow, resulting in a backlog of talents.
Therefore, it is requested to select people with an official rank of more than 200 shi, and those with a hundred shi who are proficient in one kind of Confucian classics, to be promoted to the left and right internal history, and the history of the generals; to select people with an official rank of less than 100 shi to make up the history of the prefects and governors of counties: each county is allowed to have two people, and the border counties are allowed to have one person.
Prioritize the selection of people who are familiar with the scriptures and can recite them in large quantities. If there are not enough people, select officials with anecdotes to supplement the two thousand stones. Select officials with literary anecdotes to supplement the county and state, and complete the personnel.
Please record these in the regulations for selecting academic officers.
Others still follow the law.
The emperor's instructions said: "It's accurate."
From then on, there were many well-educated Confucian scholars among the officials and ordinary officials.
Duke Shen is from the state of Lu.
When Emperor Gaozu passed through the state of Lu, Duke Shen followed his teacher as a disciple to visit him in the Nangong of the state of Lu.
During the period of Empress Dowager Lu, Shen Gong went to Chang'an to study and study, and he and Liu Ying studied under their teacher Fuqiu Bo.
After graduation, Liu Ying was granted the title of King of Chu and asked Shen Gong to be the teacher of his crown prince Liu Wu.
Liu Wu was not good at studying and hated Duke Shen. When Liu Ying, the king of Chu, died and Liu Wu became the king of Chu, he imprisoned Duke Shen.
Duke Shen felt humiliated, so he returned to Lu and taught at home. He never left home for the rest of his life and refused all guests. He went only when Liu Yu, King Gong of Lu, invited him.
More than a hundred disciples came from far away to study with him.
Professor Shen Gong only explained the meaning of words in the Book of Songs, but did not elaborate on the meaning of the scriptures. If there were any doubts, he would leave them out and not forcefully teach them.
After Wang Zang Xiangshen, a native of Lanling, studied "Poetry", he used it to serve Emperor Xiaojing and became the prince's young master. Later, he was dismissed and left the court.
As soon as the emperor ascended the throne, Wang Zang wrote a letter requesting to enter the palace and serve as the emperor's guard. He was promoted continuously and became a doctor within a year.
Zhao Wan, a native of Dai, also studied the Book of Songs from Duke Shen, and he became a royal censor.
Zhao Wan and Wang Zang asked the emperor for instructions and wanted to build a bright hall to convene the princes to hold court meetings. They could not persuade the emperor to agree to this matter, so they recommended their teacher Shen Gong.
So the emperor sent envoys carrying valuable gifts of silk and jade, driving horses and chariots to greet Duke Shen. Two disciples, Zhao Wan and Wang Zang, accompanied him in ordinary post cars.
Duke Shen came to the capital to pay homage to the emperor.
The emperor asked him about the security of the country. Shen Gong was already over eighty years old at the time. He was very old. He replied: People in power don't need to talk much, they just focus on how well they do their best.
At this time, the emperor was fond of rhetoric and rhetoric. Seeing that Shen Gong answered correctly, he was silently unhappy.
However, Shen Gong had been recruited to the court and made him the official of Taizhong. He lived in Lu's mansion and discussed the construction of Mingtang.
The Empress Dowager Dou liked the teachings of Lao Tzu and disliked Confucianism. She found out the faults of Zhao Wan and Wang Zang to blame the emperor. Therefore, the emperor stopped discussing the construction of Mingtang and handed Zhao Wan and Wang Zang to the judge for trial. Later, they both committed suicide.
Duke Shen also excused himself from official duties due to illness and returned to the state of Lu, where he died a few years later.
Among Shen Gong's disciples, there were more than a dozen people who became doctors: Kong An was appointed as the governor of Linhuai, Zhou Ba was appointed as the internal historian of Jiaoxi, Xia Kuan was appointed as the internal historian of Chengyang, Danglu was appointed as the governor of Donghai, Miao Sheng from Lanling was appointed as the governor of Changsha, Xu Yan was appointed as the lieutenant of Jiaoxi, and Quemen Qingji from Zou was appointed as the internal historian of Jiaodong.
They manage officials and people with integrity and integrity, and people praise them for their studiousness.
Although the conduct of other disciples who are academic officials is not perfect, there are also more than a hundred people who are officials, doctors, and acquaintances.
Although their explanations of the Book of Songs are different. But most of them follow Shen Gong's opinion.
Yuan Gusheng, the Taifu of King Liu Cheng of Qinghe, was a native of Qi.
Because of his study of the Book of Songs, he became a doctor during the reign of Emperor Xiaojing.
He and Huang Sheng argued in front of Emperor Jing.
Huang Sheng said: King Tang and King Wu did not succeed to the throne according to destiny, but committed regicide and usurped the throne.
Yuan Gusheng retorted: No.
In the Xia Dynasty, Jie and Yin Zhou were violent and confused, and the hearts of the people in the world all surrendered to Shang Tang and Zhou Wu. Shang Tang and Zhou Wu agreed with the wishes of the people and killed Jie and Zhou. The people of Jie and Zhou refused to serve them and turned to Tang and Wu. Tang and Wu had no choice but to establish themselves as emperors. What else is this but upholding the destiny of heaven?
Huang Sheng said: Although the hat is old, it must be worn on the head; although the shoes are new, they must be worn on the feet.
Why? This is why there is a difference between up and down.
Although Jie and Zhou were immoral, they were in the upper position as monarchs; although Tang and Wu were sages, they were in the lower position as ministers.
When the monarch makes a mistake, the ministers cannot directly advise and correct it to maintain the dignity of the emperor. Instead, they use the fault to kill the monarch and replace him as the king in the south. What is this if not regicide and usurpation of the throne?
Yuan Gusheng replied: If we have to judge right and wrong according to your statement, then is it not right for Emperor Gao to replace Qin Dynasty as the emperor?
Then Emperor Jing said: If you eat meat but not horse liver, it is not not ignorant of the delicious taste of meat; if a scholar does not talk about whether Tang and Wu are destined to succeed to the throne, it is not considered stupid.
So the argument ceased.
Since then, no scholar has dared to argue whether Tang and Wu were established by destiny or whether they exiled Jie and Zhou to usurp the monarchy.
Empress Dowager Dou liked the book "Laozi" and summoned Yuan Gusheng to ask him about his experience in reading this book.
Yuan Gusheng said: These are just ordinary people's remarks.
Empress Dowager Dou said angrily: How can it compare with Confucian poetry and books that control prisoners!
So he asked Yuan Gu to enter the animal pen and kill the wild boar.
Emperor Jing knew that the Queen Mother was angry and Yuan Gu said she was not guilty, so he lent him sharp weapons. He went down to the animal pen to stab the wild boar, and hit it right in the heart. With one stab, the wild boar fell to the ground.
The Queen Mother remained silent. There was no reason to punish him anymore, so she had to give up.
Soon after, Emperor Jing believed that Yuan Gu was honest and upright, and worshiped him as the tutor of Liu Cheng, the king of Qinghe.
After a long time, he was discharged from office due to illness.
The current emperor has just ascended the throne and recruited Yuan Gu to join the court because of his virtuous character.
Many Confucian scholars who liked to flatter Yuan Gu were jealous and slandered Yuan Gu, saying that Yuan Gu was old, so he was dismissed from office and sent home.
Yuan Gu was already over ninety years old at this time.
When he was recruited, Gongsun Hong from Xue Yi was also recruited, but he did not dare to face Yuan Gu.
Yuan Gu said to him: Mr. Gongsun, you must discuss things with upright knowledge and don't use evil theories to cater to the world.
Since then, people in Qi have always discussed the Book of Songs based on Yuan Gusheng's insights.
Some Qi people became famous because of their achievements in studying the Book of Songs. They were all disciples of Yuan Gu.
Han Sheng was from Yanjun.
During the reign of Emperor Xiaowen, he was a doctor, and during the reign of Emperor Jing, he was the tutor of Liu Shun, the king of Changshan.
Han Sheng deduced the purpose of "Shi" and wrote "Nei Zhuan" and "Wai Zhuan" with tens of thousands of words. The terms in the book are quite different from those in Qi and Lu, but the purpose is the same.
Huainan Bensheng received his inheritance from him.
From then on, people who talked about "Poetry" in Yanzhao area all followed Han Sheng's views.
Han Sheng's grandson Han Shang is a doctor appointed by the current emperor.
Fusheng was from Jinan County.
I had previously been a doctor of Qin Dynasty.
During the reign of Emperor Xiaowen, he wanted to find someone who could study "Shang Shu", but he searched all over the world and found no one. Later, he heard that Fu Shenghui taught it, so he planned to recruit him.
Fu Sheng was already over ninety years old at that time. He was very old and could not walk, so Emperor Wen ordered Taichang to send his master Chao Cuo to Fu Sheng to learn from him.
When the Qin Dynasty burned Confucian books, Fu Sheng hid "Shang Shu" in the wall.
Later, when the war broke out, Fu Sheng went into exile. After the Han Dynasty pacified the world, he returned to look for the "Shang Shu" in his collection. He had lost dozens of articles and only got twenty-nine articles. So he taught the remaining "Shang Shu" in the Qilu area.
Since then, scholars have become very good at explaining "Shang Shu", and all famous scholars east of Miaoshan have dabbled in "Shang Shu" to teach students.
Fu Sheng taught Zhang Sheng and Ouyang Sheng from Jinan, and Ouyang Sheng taught Qiancheng Ren Kuan.
After Kuan became proficient in "Shangshu", he participated in the county election based on his achievements in Confucian classics, went to study under a doctorate official, and studied under Kong Anguo.
Erkuan's family was poor and had no resources, so he often worked as a kitchen worker for the students, and often went out to work secretly to earn money to provide for his own food and clothing needs.
He often read the scriptures when he went out, and read and reviewed them aloud when he was resting.
According to the ranking of the examination results, he filled the vacancy of Yan Weishi.
At that time, Zhang Tangzheng was fond of Confucianism, so he asked Erkuan to be his agent and responsible for reporting the case. Erkuan judged difficult and major cases based on classics and ancient methods, so Zhang Tang favored him.
Erkuan was gentle and kind-hearted, with integrity and intelligence. He was able to control his own words and deeds. He was also good at writing books and drafting memorials. He was agile in writing and thinking, but he was clumsy in speech and could not explain.
Zhang Tang thought that he was a loyal person and praised him many times.
When Zhang Tang became the imperial censor, he appointed Er Kuan as an official and recommended him to the emperor.
After the emperor summoned Erkuan to inquire about him, he liked him very much.
Six years after Zhang Tang's death, Erkuan became a royal official and died after nine years in office.
Erkuan was one of the three princes. He had a long career as an official because of his gentle and kind temperament, his ability to obey the emperor's will, and his ability to mediate disputes. However, he did not correct the emperor's mistakes. During his tenure as an official, his subordinate officials looked down upon him and did not do their best for him.
Zhang Sheng also became a doctoral officer.
Fu Sheng's grandson was also recruited for studying "Shang Shu", but he could not clarify the meaning of "Shang Shu".
From then on, Zhou Ba and Kong Anguo from Lu and Jia Jia from Luoyang were all good at teaching the contents of Shangshu.
The Kong family had the Shangshu written in ancient pre-Qin script, and Kong Anguo re-copied and read them using the current official script font, thus giving rise to his own academic school.
Kong Anguo obtained more than a dozen lost chapters in "Shang Shu", and the number of "Shang Shu" probably increased gradually from this time on.
Many scholars have interpreted the "Book of Rites", but Gao Tangsheng's opinion from Lu County is the closest to the original meaning.
The Book of Rites was originally incomplete since the time of Confucius. After Qin Shihuang burned the book, more chapters of the book were lost. Today, only the Book of Rites survives, and Gao Tangsheng can explain it.
Xu Sheng of Lu State was good at practicing etiquette.
During the reign of Emperor Xiaowen, Xu Sheng served as a ceremonial official.
He passed down etiquette to his sons and grandsons Xu Yan and Xu Xiang.
Xu Xiang was naturally good at practicing etiquette, but he was not proficient in the "Book of Rites"; Xu Yan was very familiar with the "Book of Rites", but was not good at practicing etiquette.
Xu Xiang became a ceremonial official in the Han Dynasty because of his good practice of etiquette and rituals, and he served as an official in Guangling Neishi.
Xu Yan and his disciples Gonghu Yiyi, Huansheng and Shanchang all served as ceremonial officials in the Han Dynasty.
Xiao Fen, a native of Xiaqiu, became the governor of Huaiyang because he was proficient in the Book of Rites.
From then on, those who were able to explain the Book of Rites and practice etiquette and rituals were all from the Xu family.
Ever since Shang Qu of Lu State studied the Book of Changes from Confucius, after Confucius died, Shang Qu taught the Book of Changes. After six generations, it was passed down to Tian He, a native of Qi County. Tian He was named Zizhuang, and then the Han Dynasty was established.
Tian He taught it to Wang, a native of Dongwu, whose given name was Zizhong, who taught it to Yang He, a native of Shichuan.
Because Yang He was proficient in the "Book of Changes", he was recruited by the imperial court in the first year of Yuanguang and became a senior official.
Jimo, a native of Qi, was proficient in the Book of Changes and became the prime minister of Chengyang.
Meng Dan, a native of Guangchuan, became the prince's official because he was proficient in the "Book of Changes".
Zhou Ba from Lu people. Heng Hu from Ju and Fu Yan from Linyi were all due to their proficiency in the Book of Changes.
But the most accurate teaching of the "Book of Changes" comes from the teachings of Yang He's family.
Dong Zhongshu is from Guangchuan County.
Because of his study of "Spring and Autumn", he became a doctor during the reign of Emperor Xiaojing.
He taught at home, and many people came to learn from him, so he could not teach them one by one in person. Instead, his disciples passed it on from generation to generation, and some of them had never even met him in person. Dong Zhongshu stayed at home and never visited the garden next to his house for three years. He was so dedicated to his studies.
His appearance and behavior when he came in and out were all in line with etiquette. The students followed his example and respected him.
After the current emperor ascended the throne, he served as the Prime Minister of Jiangdu.
He deduced the reasons for the alternating movement of yin and yang based on the changes in natural disasters and special phenomena recorded in the Spring and Autumn Annals. Therefore, when praying for rain, he shut down all kinds of yang qi and released all kinds of yin qi, and when he stopped rain, he used the opposite method.
This approach was implemented in Jiangdu State and all achieved the expected results.
Later, he was demoted to a middle doctor and wrote "The Chronicle of Disasters" at home.
At this time, a fire broke out in the Gaodi Temple in Liaodong. The master's father Yan was jealous of him, so he stole his book and wrote it to the emperor.
The emperor summoned the Confucian scholars and showed them the book. The Confucian scholars believed that it contained accusations and ridicules of the government.
Dong Zhongshu's student Lu Bushu didn't know that this was his teacher's work and thought it was extremely stupid. So he handed Dong Zhongshu over to the judge and sentenced him to death. However, the emperor issued an edict and pardoned him.
Therefore, Dong Zhongshu never dared to talk about the theory of disasters.
Dong Zhongshu is honest and upright.
During this period, the imperial court was using troops to eliminate the intrusion of ethnic minorities inside and outside the four borders. Gongsun Hong's research on "Spring and Autumn" was not as successful as Dong Zhongshu's, but he was good at catering to the secular world, so he was able to occupy a high position and became a minister.
Dong Zhongshu thought that Gongsun Hong was a flatterer.
Gongsun Hong hated him and told the emperor: Only Dong Zhongshu can serve as the prime minister of King Jiaoxi.
The King of Jiaoxi was vicious and violent, but he had always heard that Dong Zhongshu was virtuous and treated him well.
Dong Zhongshu was afraid that he would get into trouble if he stayed in an official position for a long time, so he resigned and went home.
Until his death, he never managed private property, but devoted himself to researching knowledge and writing treatises.
Therefore, since the founding of the Han Dynasty, it has gone through five dynasties. During this period, only Dong Zhongshu was the most proficient in the Spring and Autumn Annals and had a high reputation. He taught Gongyang Studies in the Spring and Autumn Annals.
Hu Wusheng was from Qijun.
During the reign of Emperor Xiaojing, he became a doctor. Later, he returned to his hometown to teach "Spring and Autumn" due to his old age.
Many people who discussed "Spring and Autumn" in Qi were taught by Hu Wusheng, and Gongsun Hong also received his teachings.
Jiang Sheng, a native of Xiaqiu, studied Guliang studies in the Spring and Autumn Annals.
Since Gongsun Hong was put in high office, he had collected and compared the scriptures and meanings of Guliang School and Gongyang School, and finally adopted Gongyang's theory taught by Dong Zhongshu.
The most successful disciples of Dong Zhongshu were: Chu Da from Lanling, Yin Zhong from Guangchuan, and Lu Bushu from Wen.
Chu Daguan became Prime Minister of Liang Kingdom.
Lu Bushu, who was a long-time official, went out as an envoy with a talisman in hand to decide the crime of rebellion by Liu An, the king of Huainan. He dared to judge the princes and kings on his own without asking for instructions. He judged the case fairly according to the "Spring and Autumn" classics, and the emperor thought it was right.
Among the disciples, those who have good official career have achieved the position of great officials; there are more than a hundred people who pay homage to him and have anecdotes.
Dong Zhongshu's sons and grandsons also became high-ranking officials because of their proficiency in Confucianism.
summary:
Tai Shigong reflects on the decline of the Zhou royal family, which led to the emergence of satirical poems like "Guan Ju." Confucius, troubled by the decay of the royal way and the rise of evil, compiled the "Poems" and "Books," organized ritual music, and became so captivated by the "Shao" music in Qi that he forgot the taste of meat for months. After returning to Lu from Wei, he corrected the music, restoring order to the "Ya" and "Song." Despite traveling to over seventy states seeking office, no one employed him. When a unicorn was hunted in Lu, Confucius lamented his unrealized ideals and wrote the "Spring and Autumn Annals" based on Lu's historical records to express imperial laws.

After Confucius's death, his seventy-plus disciples dispersed, some becoming teachers to princes, others teaching scholars, and some living in seclusion. Zilu served in Wei, Zizhang in Chen, Ziyu in Chu, Zixia taught in Xihe, and Zigong died in Qi. Figures like Tian Zifang and Wu Qi were trained by Zixia's generation. Confucianism declined during the Warring States, squeezed out by warring powers, but persisted in Qi and Lu. Under Kings Wei and Xuan of Qi, Mencius and Xunzi advanced Confucius's work. The Qin dynasty burned books and killed scholars, leaving the Six Arts incomplete. Chen She's rebellion saw Lu Confucians carrying Confucius's ritual vessels to him, and Kong Jia became his doctor, dying with him. Despite Chen She's brief rule, scholars followed him due to resentment over Qin's book burning. Even when Han Gaozu besieged Lu, Confucians continued reciting scriptures and practicing rituals.

After the Han dynasty's establishment, Confucian scholars revived their studies. Shusun Tong formulated Han rituals, becoming Taichang, and his disciples became court officials, sparking interest in Confucianism. However, wars delayed school establishment. Under Emperors Xiaohui, Empress Lu, Xiaowen, and Xiaojing, Confucianism was sidelined, with only a few doctorates retained. When the current emperor ascended, Zhao Wan and Wang Zang promoted Confucianism, leading to the recommendation of virtuous scholars. Teachers of the "Shi" included Shen Peigong in Lu, Yuan Yinsheng in Qi, and Han Taifu in Yan. "Shangshu" was taught by Fusheng in Jinan, "Li" by Gaotangsheng in Lu, and "Spring and Autumn" by Hu Wusheng in Qi and Dong Zhongshu in Zhao. After Empress Dowager Dou's death, Tian Fu became prime minister, favoring Confucianism, and Gongsun Hong rose through his expertise in "Spring and Autumn," inspiring widespread study.

Gongsun Hong, as prime minister, petitioned the emperor to promote education, citing ancient practices of village schools. He proposed assigning fifty disciples to doctors, exempting them from taxes, and selecting students from commoners and local officials. After a year of study, those mastering one classic could fill official vacancies, with exceptional students reported directly. Those failing were dismissed, and their recommenders punished. The emperor approved, leading to many educated Confucian scholars among officials.

Shen Peigong of Lu taught the "Shi" without elaborating on meanings, leaving doubts unforced. His disciples included Wang Zang and Zhao Wan, who recommended him to the emperor. Shen Gong, over eighty, advised the emperor to focus on actions over words, but the emperor, fond of rhetoric, was displeased. After Empress Dowager Dou's interference led to Zhao Wan and Wang Zang's deaths, Shen Gong retired and died. His disciples became doctors and officials, praised for their integrity.

Yuan Gusheng of Qi debated Huang Sheng before Emperor Jing about whether Tang and Wu usurped the throne. Yuan argued they followed heaven's mandate, while Huang insisted on hierarchical propriety. Emperor Jing ended the debate. When Empress Dowager Dou questioned Yuan about "Laozi," he dismissed it as common talk, angering her. She ordered him to kill a wild boar, which he did with the emperor's help. Later, he became tutor to King Qinghe but retired due to illness. Recruited by the current emperor, he was slandered and sent home at over ninety, advising Gongsun Hong to uphold uprightness.

Han Sheng of Yan, a doctor under Emperor Xiaowen, wrote "Nei Zhuan" and "Wai Zhuan" on the "Shi," with terms differing from Qi and Lu but similar purposes. His grandson Han Shang became a doctor.

Fusheng of Jinan, a former Qin doctor, hid "Shangshu" during the book burning. After the Han, he recovered twenty-nine chapters and taught them. His students included Zhang Sheng and Ouyang Sheng, who taught Erkuan. Erkuan, despite poverty, studied diligently, became an official, and served under Zhang Tang, rising to a high position but failing to correct the emperor's mistakes. Fusheng's grandson also studied "Shangshu" but couldn't clarify it. Kong Anguo re-copied ancient "Shangshu" in modern script, adding lost chapters.

Gaotangsheng of Lu best interpreted the "Book of Rites," which was incomplete after Qin's burning. Xu Sheng of Lu practiced rituals, passing them to his sons Xu Yan and Xu Xiang. Xu Xiang excelled in practice, while Xu Yan knew the text. Their disciples became ceremonial officials. Tian He of Qi taught the "Book of Changes," passed down from Confucius through six generations. His teachings influenced Yang He, who became a senior official.

Dong Zhongshu of Guangchuan, a doctor under Emperor Xiaojing, taught "Spring and Autumn" with such dedication that he didn't visit his garden for three years. He served as Prime Minister of Jiangdu, using yin-yang theory for rain prayers. After a fire at Gaodi Temple, his book on disasters was seen as accusatory, and he was nearly executed but pardoned. He resigned as Prime Minister of Jiaoxi due to fear of trouble, dying at home. He was the most proficient in "Spring and Autumn" during the Han, teaching Gongyang studies. His disciples included Chu Da, Yin Zhong, and Lu Bushu, who judged cases fairly.
rel_path: 史记/七十列传/儒林列传

translation:
Liu Jing was a native of Qi. In the fifth year of Emperor Gao of the Han Dynasty, he went to Longxi to guard the frontier and passed by Luoyang, where Emperor Gao was living at that time.
After Liu Jing entered the city, he took off the crossbar used to pull the cart, put on a sheepskin coat, and paid a visit to General Yu from Qi State. He said: I hope to see the emperor to talk about major national affairs.
General Yu wanted to give him a fresh and good clothes to change into. Liu Jing said: I came wearing silk clothes, so I went to see him in silk clothes;
So General Yu went to the palace to report Liu Jing's request to the emperor.
The emperor summoned Liu Jing to the palace and gave him food.
After a while, the emperor asked Liu Jing what important matter he wanted to discuss, and Liu Jing said: Your Majesty established Luoyang as the capital, is it to compete with the Zhou Dynasty for prosperity? The emperor said: Yes.
Liu Jing said: Your Majesty's control of the world is different from that of the Zhou Dynasty. The ancestors of the Zhou Dynasty started from Houji, and Yao granted him the title of Tai. He accumulated virtues and good deeds for more than ten generations.
Gong Liu came to live in Bin to avoid Xia Jie's tyranny.
Because of the intrusion of the Di tribe, King Tai left Bindi and drove his animals to live in Qishan. The people in the country rushed to follow him.
When King Wen of Zhou became the leader of the Western princes, he properly resolved the dispute between Yu and Rui, and from then on he became a man destined to rule the world. The capable men Lu Wang and Boyi returned from the sea and joined him.
When King Wu of Zhou attacked Yin Zhou, the 800 princes who came to Mengjin to form an alliance without making an appointment. Everyone said that Yin Zhou was ready to attack Yin Zhou, so the Yin Shang Dynasty was destroyed.
King Cheng of Zhou came to the throne, and the Duke of Zhou and others assisted him. He built the city of Zhou in Luoyi and made it the center of the world. The princes from all over the world came to pay tribute and taxes, and the distances were equal. In this way, if a monarch is virtuous, it will be easy for him to become king and rule the world; if he is not virtuous, it will be easy for him to perish.
Anyone who established their capital here wanted to use virtuous governance to inspire the people like the Zhou Dynasty, and did not want to rely on the dangerous natural situation to allow future generations of monarchs to abuse the people with their extravagance.
During the heyday of the Zhou Dynasty, the world was harmonious, and all the tribes in the four directions were attracted to Luoyi, attached themselves to the Zhou Dynasty, admired the morality of Lord Zhou, and were grateful for his kindness. They attached themselves to and served the Emperor of Zhou together, and no soldiers were stationed in defense or sent to fight. All the people of the big countries in the eight directions surrendered and submitted, and they all contributed tributes and taxes.
When the Zhou Dynasty declined and was divided into two small kingdoms, the Western Zhou Dynasty and the Eastern Zhou Dynasty, no one in the world came to worship, and the Zhou Dynasty could no longer control the world.
It's not that its kindness is too little, but that its situation is too weak.
Now your Majesty has risen from Pei County, Fengyi, and gathered three thousand soldiers. He led them directly into battle and swept through the Shu and Han regions. He pacified the Three Qin Dynasties. He fought with Xiang Yu in Xingyang to compete for the danger of Chenggao. He fought seventy battles and forty small battles, causing the blood of the people of the world to flow on the earth. , the withered bones of father and son were exposed in the wilderness, countless corpses were scattered in the fields, miserable cries were heard endlessly, and people with injuries and disabilities were unable to move. However, this situation is compared with the prosperous period of King Cheng and King Kang of Zhou Dynasty. I privately think that this is not the same.
Besides, Qin is covered by high mountains and surrounded by the Yellow River. The border fortresses on all sides can serve as a solid line of defense. Even if there is a sudden crisis, millions of soldiers can be ready for a battle.
Relying on Qin's original management foundation and relying on fertile land, this is the so-called land of heaven with dangerous situation and abundant products.
Your Majesty entered Hangu Pass and built the capital there. Even if there is trouble in Shandong, the original land of Qin can be preserved and occupied.
When fighting with others, you cannot completely win without grabbing him by the throat and hitting him on the back.
If your Majesty enters Hangu Pass and builds a capital, he will control the original areas of Qin. This is to grab the world by its throat and hit it on the back.
Emperor Gao of the Han Dynasty sought the opinions of his ministers, who were all from the Shandong area. They argued one after another that the Zhou Dynasty established its capital in Luoyang and ruled the world for hundreds of years, while the Qin Dynasty established its capital in Guan Nei and perished after only two generations. It was better to establish its capital in the capital of the Zhou Dynasty.
The emperor hesitated.
After Zhang Liang, the remaining marquis, clearly explained the favorable conditions for entering Guanzhong and establishing the capital, the emperor took a car westward to establish the capital in Guanzhong that day.
At that time, the emperor said: Originally it was Liu Jing who advocated establishing the capital in Qin, and Lou is Liu.
So he changed Liu Jing's surname to Liu, granted him the official position of doctor, and gave him the title Fengchun Jun.
In the seventh year of Emperor Gao of the Han Dynasty, King Xin of Han rebelled, and Emperor Gao of the Han Dynasty personally went to defeat him.
When he arrived in Jinyang, he learned that Han Wangxin was colluding with the Xiongnu to attack the Han Dynasty. The emperor was furious and sent envoys to the Xiongnu.
The Xiongnu hid their strong and capable soldiers and their fat cattle and horses, leaving only old and weak soldiers and thin livestock exposed.
More than ten batches of envoys were sent back, and they all said that the Huns could attack.
The emperor sent Liu Jing to go on an envoy to the Xiongnu again. He came back and reported: The two countries are at war, and it is time to show off their strengths.
Now when I go there, I see only emaciated livestock and old and weak soldiers. This must be a deliberate attempt to show shortcomings and ambush surprise troops to win.
I think the Huns cannot attack.
By this time, the Han army had crossed Gouzhu Mountain, and an army of 200,000 soldiers had set out to fight.
The emperor was very angry after hearing Liu Jing's words and scolded Liu Jing: "Qi is a coward!"
I got the official position by relying on my words, but now I dare to stop me from sending troops by talking nonsense.
Liu Jing was imprisoned in shackles and imprisoned in Guangwu County.
Emperor Gao led his army to Pingcheng, and when they arrived at Pingcheng, the Huns unexpectedly sent out troops to siege Emperor Gao on Baishan Mountain. They were besieged for seven days before they were able to relieve the siege.
When Emperor Gao returned to Guangwu County, he pardoned Liu Jing and said to Liu Jing: I didn't listen to your opinion, so I was besieged in Pingcheng.
I have beheaded all the ten people who were approved by the Huns to attack.
So Liu Jing was rewarded with a settlement of 2,000 households, and was granted the title of Marquis of Guannei, also known as Marquis of Jianxin.
Emperor Gao of the Han Dynasty withdrew from Pingcheng and returned to the court, while King Xin of Han fled to Hu Di.
At this time, Maodun was the Xiongnu Chanyu. He had a powerful army with 300,000 warriors and repeatedly invaded the northern border.
The emperor was very worried about this situation and asked Liu Jing what to do.
Liu Jing said: The Han Dynasty had just settled down, and the soldiers were tired of fighting. They could not use force to subdue the Xiongnu.
Maodun killed his father and proclaimed himself a Chanyu, and took many of his father's concubines as his wives. He relied on force to establish his power, but he could not be persuaded by benevolence, righteousness, and morality.
We can only make long-term plans to make his descendants surrender to the Han Dynasty, but I'm afraid your Majesty can't do it.
The emperor said: If it is really feasible, why can't it be done?
What to do?
Liu Jing replied: If your majesty can marry the eldest princess born to the empress to Moton as his wife, and give him rich gifts. He knows that it is the daughter of the empress of the Han Dynasty who has sent rich gifts. The rough foreigners will definitely envy the use of Han women as Yan family members, and the son they will give birth to will definitely be the prince and will succeed the throne in the future.
Why do this?
Because the Xiongnu coveted the Han Dynasty's generous wealth and gifts.
His Majesty repeatedly inquired about and presented gifts to the Han Dynasty that were superfluous throughout the year but rare to the Xiongnu. He also sent eloquent people to enlighten him with etiquette.
When Maodun reigns, of course he is the son-in-law of the Han Dynasty; after his death, his grandson of the Han Dynasty is the monarch.
Who has ever heard of a grandson who dared to stand up to his grandfather?
The army could gradually make the Xiongnu surrender without going to war.
If His Majesty cannot send the eldest princess, but lets a royal woman or a concubine pretend to be a princess, he will know about it and will not respect her and get close to her. There will be no good in that.
After hearing this, Emperor Gao said: OK.
He wanted to send the eldest princess to the Huns.
